name: Arabic

formality.description: Arabic formality operates through register choice (MSA vs colloquial) and pronoun/verb forms. Modern Standard Arabic (فصحى) is the written standard across all Arabic-speaking regions. Colloquial dialects vary widely by region but are not typically used in software UI. Within MSA, formality is expressed through vocabulary choice and sentence structure rather than a T-V pronoun distinction.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Arabic has grammatical gender on nouns, verbs, and adjectives. When addressing users of unknown gender, use masculine plural as the conventional default, or restructure sentences to avoid gendered forms where natural.

msa-professional.label: فصحى Professional (MSA)
msa-professional.description: Standard for software UI, websites, and business. Modern Standard Arabic with clear, professional vocabulary.
msa-professional.prompt: Modern Standard Arabic (فصحى). Professional register suitable for software interfaces, websites, and business communication. Use clear, accessible vocabulary — avoid overly literary or archaic phrasing.

msa-formal.label: فصحى Formal (MSA)
msa-formal.description: For government, legal, academic, or official contexts requiring elevated register.
msa-formal.prompt: Formal Modern Standard Arabic (فصحى). Elevated register for government, legal, and academic contexts. Use precise terminology and complex sentence structures appropriate for official documentation.

notes: Arabic dialects (Egyptian, Levantine, Gulf, Maghrebi) are significantly different from MSA but don't have separate ISO 639-1 codes. Dialect-specific translation would require custom register prompts.

linguisticChallenges.msaDialectMixing: MT models frequently produce output that mixes MSA with dialectal Arabic (especially Egyptian or Levantine). In a single paragraph, an MSA verb might appear alongside a colloquial conjunction. This is immediately visible to Arabic readers and reads as incoherent.
linguisticChallenges.rootPatternMorphology: Arabic words derive from three-consonant roots through vowel patterns (e.g., k-t-b → كتب wrote, كتاب book, مكتبة library, كاتب writer). MT must understand that morphologically related forms share semantic connections but have distinct meanings.
linguisticChallenges.bidirectionalText: RTL Arabic with embedded LTR content (variables, URLs, code, numbers) creates complex bidirectional text rendering. Placeholder insertion must respect Unicode bidi algorithm rules. Strings like '{count} ملفات' require careful handling to prevent visual reordering errors.
linguisticChallenges.diacritics: Written Arabic usually omits short vowel diacritics (tashkeel/حركات), creating ambiguity — 'كتب' could be 'wrote' (kataba), 'books' (kutub), or 'was written' (kutiba). MT must disambiguate from context without generating unnecessary diacritics in output (they look pedagogical or Qur'anic in UI text).
linguisticChallenges.genderAgreement: Verbs, adjectives, and demonstratives must agree in gender with their noun. Getting this wrong is one of the most common MT errors in Arabic — e.g., هذه الملف (feminine demonstrative + masculine noun) is immediately wrong.
linguisticChallenges.dualNumber: Arabic distinguishes singular, dual (exactly two), and plural. The dual form affects nouns, adjectives, verbs, and pronouns. This is reflected in the 6 plural categories (zero, one, two, few, many, other). ICU MessageFormat must account for all six.
linguisticChallenges.complexPlurals: Arabic broken plurals (جمع التكسير) change the internal vowel pattern of the word rather than adding a suffix. There is no predictable rule — each noun has its own plural form (كتاب → كتب, قلم → أقلام). MT systems sometimes apply regular plural suffixes instead of the correct broken plural.

dialects.classification: Modern Standard Arabic (MSA) for written/formal use; regional vernaculars are mutually unintelligible between distant groups

encyclopedic.history: Arabic spread with the Islamic conquests of the 7th-8th centuries. MSA is the standardized literary form based on Classical Arabic, used in media, education, and formal contexts across all Arabic-speaking countries. It is one of the six official UN languages.

formality.description: Bengali has a three-tier pronoun system: আপনি (apni, formal/respectful), তুমি (tumi, familiar), and তুই (tui, intimate/condescending). Software UI uses আপনি. Bengali also distinguishes between শুদ্ধ ভাষা (shuddho bhasha, standard/literary) and চলিত ভাষা (cholito bhasha, colloquial). Modern software uses cholito bhasha.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Bengali pronouns are gender-neutral. Third-person pronouns (সে/তিনি) do not distinguish gender.

professional.label: চলিত (Professional)
professional.description: Standard for software UI. Colloquial standard (চলিত ভাষা) with আপনি address.
professional.prompt: Professional Bengali (চলিত ভাষা/cholito bhasha). Use আপনি (apni) for respectful second-person address. Modern colloquial standard — not literary/sadhu bhasha. Clear, accessible vocabulary appropriate for software interfaces.

formal-shuddho.label: শুদ্ধ (Formal/Literary)
formal-shuddho.description: For government, academic, or literary contexts using standard literary Bengali.
formal-shuddho.prompt: Formal literary Bengali (শুদ্ধ ভাষা/shuddho bhasha). Elevated register with literary verb forms. For government, academic, and formal institutional contexts.

linguisticChallenges.scriptComplexity: Bengali script (বাংলা) uses conjunct consonants where multiple consonants merge into single glyphs. Incorrect rendering or font issues can make text illegible. MT output must produce valid Unicode sequences that render correctly across platforms.
linguisticChallenges.formalPronounSystem: Bengali has three register levels via pronouns: তুই (tui, very intimate/rude), তুমি (tumi, familiar), আপনি (apni, formal). Software UI must use আপনি consistently. Using তুই in a professional context is offensive.
linguisticChallenges.verbInflection: Bengali verbs inflect for tense, person, and formality level, creating complex paradigms. The same verb has different forms for আপনি vs তুমি vs তুই address levels, and MT must maintain consistency.
linguisticChallenges.postpositions: Like Hindi, Bengali uses postpositions. Variable-containing strings must account for postpositional placement after nouns.
linguisticChallenges.banglaVsBangladeshi: Standard Bengali (based on the Nadia dialect) is used in both West Bengal (India) and Bangladesh, but vocabulary and usage differ — particularly for technical terms. Software typically targets the larger Bangladeshi audience unless specifically for India.

dialects.classification: Standard Bengali (চলিত ভাষা) based on Nadia dialect. Regional variation between Bangladesh and West Bengal in vocabulary and some pronunciation.

encyclopedic.history: Bengali is the 7th most spoken language in the world. It was central to the Language Movement of 1952 in East Pakistan (now Bangladesh), which is commemorated as International Mother Language Day by UNESCO.

formality.description: Bulgarian has a T-V distinction: Вие (Vie, formal/plural) vs ти (ti, informal/singular). Professional software uses Вие-form.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Bulgarian has grammatical gender. When gender is unknown, masculine is the conventional default.

formal-vie.label: Вие (Formal)
formal-vie.description: Standard for professional software and business communication.
formal-vie.prompt: Formal Bulgarian. Use Вие-form for second-person address. Professional register for software interfaces.

informal-ti.label: Ти (Informal)
informal-ti.description: For consumer apps, social media, and casual contexts.
informal-ti.prompt: Informal Bulgarian. Use ти-form. Conversational tone for consumer products.

linguisticChallenges.definiteSuffix: Bulgarian marks definiteness with a suffix (-ът/-ат, -та, -то, -те) attached to the first nominal element. This is unique among Slavic languages. MT must correctly place the definite article suffix rather than producing a separate article word.
linguisticChallenges.noCaseSystem: Bulgarian is the only major Slavic language that has lost its case system (except for pronouns). This simplifies some aspects but means word order carries more semantic weight than in Russian or Polish.
linguisticChallenges.cliticDoubling: Bulgarian uses clitic pronoun doubling where both a clitic and full pronoun appear: 'На мен ми хареса' (To me, it-to-me pleased = I liked it). MT from English often produces only one or the other, sounding unnatural.
linguisticChallenges.renarration: Bulgarian has a unique renarrative mood (преизказно наклонение) used for hearsay or unwitnessed events. There is no equivalent in English or most other languages, making it a distinctive translation challenge.

dialects.classification: Standard Bulgarian based on eastern dialects. Western dialects are transitional toward Macedonian.

encyclopedic.history: Bulgarian is the oldest documented Slavic language. The Cyrillic alphabet was developed in the First Bulgarian Empire in the 9th century and later spread to Russia, Serbia, and other Slavic peoples.

fsts.name: spaCy (no dedicated Bulgarian model; use multilingual)

name: Canadian French

formality.description: Canadian French follows the same T-V system as France French but Québécois usage trends more toward tu-form in everyday contexts, including some software. The vocabulary differs significantly from France French (e.g., 'courriel' not 'e-mail', 'magasiner' not 'faire du shopping').

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Quebec has been at the forefront of écriture inclusive in French. The Office québécois de la langue française promotes gender-inclusive writing. Doublet forms ('les utilisateurs et les utilisatrices') or truncated forms ('les utilisateur·rice·s') are both accepted.

professional-vous.label: Vouvoiement (Professional)
professional-vous.description: Standard for software targeting Quebec and Canadian French speakers.
professional-vous.prompt: Professional Canadian French (Québécois). Use vous-form. Québécois vocabulary — use 'courriel' not 'e-mail', 'télécharger' not 'downloader'.

casual-tu.label: Tutoiement (Casual)
casual-tu.description: For consumer apps and casual Québécois contexts.
casual-tu.prompt: Casual Canadian French. Use tu-form. Québécois vocabulary and tone. Natural conversational style.

notes: Québécois French has significant vocabulary and idiomatic differences from France French.

linguisticChallenges.quebecVocabulary: Quebec French has distinct vocabulary from France French — 'char' (car, vs 'voiture'), 'magasinage' (shopping, vs 'shopping'), 'courriel' (email, vs 'email/e-mail'). Quebec French often creates French neologisms for English terms that France French borrows directly.
linguisticChallenges.tuVsVous: Quebec French uses 'tu' more broadly than France French — 'tu' is standard in most consumer contexts. The vous/tu threshold is lower.
linguisticChallenges.sacres: Quebec French has a unique set of profanity derived from Catholic liturgical terms (tabernac, câlice, ostie). While not relevant for professional UI, MT models trained on informal Quebec text sometimes produce these inappropriately.
linguisticChallenges.officeDeLaLangue: The Office québécois de la langue française (OQLF) actively promotes French terminology over English loanwords, which affects technical vocabulary choices.

dialects.classification: Quebec French (français québécois) is the standard for Canadian French. Acadian French is a distinct variety spoken in New Brunswick.

encyclopedic.history: Canadian French developed largely independently from European French after British conquest in 1763. The Quiet Revolution (1960s) and Quebec's language laws (notably Bill 101, 1977) strengthened French in public life.

foundations.name: Office québécois de la langue française

name: Chinese (Simplified)

formality.description: Chinese does not have a T-V pronoun distinction — 你 (nǐ) is standard for 'you' and 您 (nín) is the respectful form but rarely used in software UI. Formality in Chinese is expressed through vocabulary choice, sentence structure complexity, and the use of four-character idioms (成语 chéngyǔ). Software UI uses clear, modern Mandarin with no classical Chinese phrasing.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Spoken Chinese pronouns are gender-neutral (tā). Written Chinese distinguishes 他 (he), 她 (she), 它 (it), but gender-neutral 他 is conventionally used for unknown gender. In UI text, prefer restructuring to avoid third-person pronouns entirely.

professional.label: 专业 (Professional)
professional.description: Standard for software UI, websites, and business communication in mainland China.
professional.prompt: Professional Simplified Chinese (简体中文). Clear, modern Mandarin appropriate for software interfaces and business communication. Use 你 (not 您) for second person — 您 is overly deferential for most UI contexts. Avoid classical Chinese phrasing and four-character idioms unless they're commonly understood. Keep sentences concise.

formal.label: 正式 (Formal)
formal.description: For government, enterprise, legal, or official contexts where elevated language is expected.
formal.prompt: Formal Simplified Chinese. Use 您 for respectful second-person address. Elevated register appropriate for government portals, legal text, and enterprise communication. Precise vocabulary, longer sentence structures acceptable.

casual.label: 轻松 (Casual)
casual.description: For consumer apps, social media, gaming, and youth-oriented products.
casual.prompt: Casual Simplified Chinese. Conversational, friendly tone for consumer products. Short sentences, modern internet-influenced vocabulary acceptable. Emoji-adjacent language style.

notes: This is Simplified Chinese (used in mainland China, Singapore). See zh-TW.json for Traditional Chinese (Taiwan, Hong Kong).

linguisticChallenges.tokenization: Chinese has no spaces between words, making tokenization non-trivial. Word boundaries are ambiguous even for native speakers — '下雨天留客天留我不留' can be segmented multiple ways with different meanings. This affects how MT systems handle compound terms and placeholder insertion.
linguisticChallenges.measureWords: Chinese requires measure words (量词/classifiers) between numbers and nouns — 一个人 (one + generic + person), 一条路 (one + long-thin + road), 一把椅子 (one + graspable + chair). Using the generic 个 for everything sounds uneducated. MT systems frequently default to 个.
linguisticChallenges.simplifiedTraditionalConfusion: Models sometimes produce Traditional characters (繁體字) when Simplified (简体字) is requested, or vice versa. Single-character substitutions can slip through — e.g., 裡 (Traditional) vs 里 (Simplified). This is especially common with technical terms and proper nouns.
linguisticChallenges.registerVocabulary: Formality is expressed through vocabulary choice, not grammar. 你 (nǐ, casual you) vs 您 (nín, respectful you) is the main pronoun distinction. Four-character idioms (成语) convey erudition but can be opaque — use them sparingly in UI text. '请' (please) at sentence start marks formal register.
linguisticChallenges.aspectMarkers: Chinese expresses time through aspect markers (了 completion, 过 experience, 着 ongoing), not verb tense. Translating English past/present/future tense literally produces awkward Chinese. MT often inserts unnecessary temporal markers.
linguisticChallenges.fullwidthPunctuation: Chinese uses full-width punctuation (，。！？) rather than half-width (,.!?). Mixing half-width and full-width punctuation in the same text is a common MT error that is immediately visible to readers.

dialects.classification: Mandarin (Standard Chinese/普通话) based on Beijing dialect; mutually unintelligible dialect groups exist (Wu, Cantonese, Min, etc.) but share the writing system

encyclopedic.history: Simplified Chinese characters were promulgated by the PRC government in the 1950s-60s to increase literacy. The traditional characters remain in use in Taiwan, Hong Kong, and Macau. Both systems share the same grammar and vocabulary.

name: Chinese (Traditional)

formality.description: Traditional Chinese formality follows similar patterns to Simplified but with distinct vocabulary choices. Taiwan Mandarin uses 您 (nín) less frequently than mainland Mandarin. Software UI uses 你 universally. Taiwan-specific vocabulary differs from mainland (e.g., 軟體 vs 软件 for software).

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Same as Simplified Chinese — written forms distinguish 他/她/它 but spoken is gender-neutral.

professional.label: 專業 (Professional)
professional.description: Standard for software UI targeting Taiwan audience.
professional.prompt: Professional Traditional Chinese (繁體中文). Taiwan Mandarin vocabulary — use Taiwan-specific terms (e.g., 軟體 not 軟件, 網路 not 網絡, 資料 not 數據). Use 你 for second person. Clear, modern language.

notes: Traditional Chinese for Taiwan. Vocabulary differs significantly from mainland Simplified Chinese — this is not just a script conversion.

linguisticChallenges.traditionalVsSimplified: Traditional Chinese uses more complex character forms than Simplified. Some characters differ substantially (e.g., 國 vs 国, 學 vs 学). MT must consistently produce Traditional characters — even a single Simplified character is immediately visible.
linguisticChallenges.taiwanVocabulary: Taiwan has distinct vocabulary from mainland China — '程式' (program, TW) vs '程序' (program, CN), '軟體' (software, TW) vs '軟件' (software, CN), '資料夾' (folder, TW) vs '文件夹' (folder, CN). These are not interchangeable.
linguisticChallenges.bopomofo: Taiwan uses Bopomofo (注音符號) for phonetic annotation rather than Pinyin. This affects input methods and user expectations around phonetic aids.
linguisticChallenges.verticalText: Traditional Chinese can be written vertically (top-to-bottom, right-to-left). While software UI uses horizontal text, some contexts (poetry, formal invitations) expect vertical layout support.

dialects.classification: Standard Mandarin as spoken in Taiwan, with Traditional Chinese characters. Taiwan Mandarin differs from mainland Mandarin in some vocabulary and usage.

encyclopedic.history: Traditional Chinese characters have been in continuous use for thousands of years. When the PRC introduced Simplified characters in the 1950s, Taiwan, Hong Kong, and Macau retained the traditional forms.

formality.description: Czech has a T-V distinction: vy (vykání, formal) vs ty (tykání, informal). Professional software uses vy-form.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Czech has grammatical gender affecting nouns, adjectives, and past-tense verbs. When gender is unknown, masculine is the conventional default. Slash notation (e.g., 'přihlášen/a') can be used where space permits.

formal-vy.label: Vykání (Formal)
formal-vy.description: Standard for professional software and business communication.
formal-vy.prompt: Formal Czech. Use vykání (vy-form) consistently. Professional register for software interfaces.

informal-ty.label: Tykání (Informal)
informal-ty.description: For consumer apps, social media, and casual contexts.
informal-ty.prompt: Informal Czech. Use tykání (ty-form). Conversational tone for consumer products.

linguisticChallenges.sevenCases: Like Polish, Czech has seven grammatical cases. Case endings affect nouns, adjectives, pronouns, and numerals. Placeholder variables in translation strings face the same challenges as in other case-heavy Slavic languages.
linguisticChallenges.háčkyAndČárky: Czech diacritics (háčky: š, č, ř, ž, ň, ď, ť and čárky: á, é, í, ó, ú, ů, ý) are essential and meaning-distinguishing. The letter ř (a raised alveolar trill) is unique to Czech and is notoriously difficult for non-native speakers.
linguisticChallenges.aspectAndMotion: Czech shares the Slavic aspect system (perfective/imperfective) and complex motion verb system with Russian and Polish, creating the same translation challenges.
linguisticChallenges.formalAddress: Czech uses Pan/Paní (similar to Polish) for formal address and vy/ty for formal/informal pronouns. The formal system is fully gendered.

dialects.classification: Standard Czech (spisovná čeština) based on central Bohemian dialect. Common Czech (obecná čeština) is a widely spoken informal koiné.

encyclopedic.history: Czech has a rich literary tradition dating to the 14th century. Jan Hus introduced diacritical marks in the 15th century that were later adopted by other Slavic languages using Latin script.

formality.description: Danish historically had a T-V distinction (De/du) but modern usage has almost entirely abandoned 'De' (formal). Software universally uses 'du'. Similar situation to Swedish and Norwegian.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Danish has common and neuter genders (en/et). For people, gender-neutral language is increasingly preferred.

professional.label: Professionel (Professional)
professional.description: Standard for software UI and business communication.
professional.prompt: Professional Danish. Use du-form (De-form is archaic). Clear, modern language appropriate for software interfaces.

linguisticChallenges.enEtGender: Danish has two grammatical genders: common (en-words) and neuter (et-words). Choosing the wrong article is conspicuous — 'en fil' (a file, common) vs 'et program' (a program, neuter).
linguisticChallenges.compoundNouns: Like German and Dutch, Danish forms compound nouns by concatenation: 'speciallægepraksisplanlægningsstabiliseringsperiode' is a valid (if extreme) word. Linking '-s-' and '-e-' rules are complex.
linguisticChallenges.stød: Danish has stød (glottal constriction) that distinguishes otherwise identical words. While invisible in writing, it affects how native speakers perceive written text and can influence MT pronunciation models.
linguisticChallenges.scandinavianMutualIntelligibility: Danish, Swedish, and Norwegian (Bokmål) are mutually intelligible to varying degrees. MT models sometimes produce Scandinavian mixing — using Norwegian vocabulary in Danish output or vice versa.

dialects.classification: Standard Danish (rigsdansk) based on the Copenhagen dialect. Regional dialects exist but are declining.

encyclopedic.history: Danish was historically the prestige language of Scandinavia and was the written language of Norway until the 19th century. Modern Norwegian Bokmål is essentially Norwegianized Danish.

formality.description: Dutch has a T-V distinction: u (formal) vs je/jij (informal). Modern Dutch software increasingly uses je-form, but enterprise and government software still uses u-form. The choice is less rigid than in German or French.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Dutch has common and neuter grammatical genders (de/het). For referring to people, the gender-neutral pronoun 'die' or 'hen/hun' is increasingly used. For UI text, prefer gender-neutral role descriptions where possible.

formal-u.label: U (Formal)
formal-u.description: Standard for professional software, enterprise, government, and institutional contexts.
formal-u.prompt: Formal Dutch. Use u-form consistently. Professional register for software interfaces, enterprise, and business communication.

informal-je.label: Je (Informal)
informal-je.description: For consumer apps, social media, startups, and casual contexts. Increasingly common in modern Dutch software.
informal-je.prompt: Informal Dutch. Use je-form. Warm, approachable tone for consumer products. This is the modern default for most Dutch consumer-facing software.

linguisticChallenges.deHetArticles: Dutch has two definite articles: 'de' (common gender) and 'het' (neuter). There is no reliable rule for which nouns take which article — it must be memorized. MT models frequently assign the wrong article, and getting it wrong is one of the most conspicuous errors to Dutch readers. This is especially problematic for technical terms and loanwords.
linguisticChallenges.separableVerbs: Dutch has separable prefix verbs where the prefix detaches in main clauses — 'opslaan' (to save) → 'Sla het bestand op' (Save the file). The prefix moves to the end of the clause. MT systems sometimes fail to separate the prefix or place it incorrectly, producing ungrammatical sentences.
linguisticChallenges.diminutives: Dutch uses diminutive suffixes (-je, -tje, -etje, -pje, -ke) extremely productively — far more than any other Germanic language. They convey smallness, affection, or casualness. 'Momentje' (just a moment), 'drankje' (a drink). Using them in formal UI is inappropriate; MT sometimes over-produces them in casual register.
linguisticChallenges.compoundSpelling: Like German, Dutch forms compounds by concatenation — but the rules for connecting elements (linking 's', linking 'en', no connector) are complex and inconsistent. 'Koninginnedag' (Queen's Day) vs 'paardenbloem' (dandelion, lit. horse-flower) vs 'zonlicht' (sunlight). MT frequently gets the connectors wrong.
linguisticChallenges.nlBeDivergence: Dutch spoken in the Netherlands vs Belgium (Flemish/Vlaams) differs in vocabulary, pronunciation, and some grammar. 'Mobile phone' is 'mobiel' (NL) vs 'gsm' (BE), 'breakfast' is 'ontbijt' (both) but 'lunch' usage varies. Software typically targets Netherlands Dutch unless specifically for Belgium.
linguisticChallenges.erConstruction: Dutch uses 'er' as a multipurpose pronoun (existential, locative, prepositional, partitive) in ways that have no direct English equivalent. 'Er zijn drie bestanden' (There are three files), 'Ik heb er twee' (I have two of them). MT from English frequently omits necessary 'er' or inserts it incorrectly.

dialects.classification: Netherlands Dutch (Netherlandic) and Belgian Dutch (Flemish/Vlaams) are the two main standard varieties. Mutually intelligible but differ in vocabulary and usage.

encyclopedic.history: Dutch is the closest major relative of English, sharing a common ancestor in West Germanic. Standard Dutch was codified in the 17th century during the Dutch Golden Age. The Taalunie (Dutch Language Union) jointly regulates the language for the Netherlands, Belgium, and Suriname.

name: Filipino (Taglish)

formality.description: Filipino formality is expressed through pronoun choice (po/ho for respect, ikaw/ka for familiar), verb forms, and the degree of English code-switching (Taglish). Software and tech communication in the Philippines routinely uses Taglish — Filipino structure with English technical terms. Pure Filipino (without English) reads as overly formal or academic for most digital contexts.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Filipino has no grammatical gender. Pronouns (siya) are gender-neutral.

taglish-professional.label: Taglish (Professional)
taglish-professional.description: Standard for apps, websites, and tech products in the Philippines. Filipino sentence structure with English for technical and UI terms.
taglish-professional.prompt: Filipino with natural Taglish code-switching. Use Filipino sentence structure but keep technical terms, UI labels, and industry-standard vocabulary in English. This matches how Filipino professionals actually read and write digital content. Use po/ho particles for polite address when contextually appropriate.

taglish-casual.label: Taglish (Casual)
taglish-casual.description: For social media, chat apps, and youth-oriented products.
taglish-casual.prompt: Casual Taglish. More English mixing, conversational tone, relaxed grammar. For social media, messaging, and apps targeting younger demographics. Drop po/ho formality particles.

formal-filipino.label: Pormal na Filipino
formal-filipino.description: Pure Filipino with minimal English. For government, educational, or cultural contexts.
formal-filipino.prompt: Formal Filipino (Pormal na Filipino). Minimize English loanwords — use Filipino equivalents where they exist. Appropriate for government, educational, and cultural institutions. Use po/ho consistently for respectful address.

notes: Google Translate uses 'tl' (Tagalog) rather than 'fil' (Filipino). The distinction is political — Filipino is the national language standardized from Tagalog. For translation purposes, they are functionally identical.

linguisticChallenges.taglish: Filipino (Tagalog) in modern tech contexts heavily code-switches with English (Taglish). The balance varies by formality — pure Filipino reads as government/academic, heavy English reads as pretentious. Software UI typically uses moderate Taglish.
linguisticChallenges.focusSystem: Tagalog has a focus/voice system where verb affixes indicate the semantic role of the topic (actor, object, location, etc.). This is fundamentally different from active/passive voice in European languages and is notoriously difficult for MT.
linguisticChallenges.affixComplexity: Tagalog uses extensive affixation (prefixes, infixes, suffixes) to derive words. The same root can produce dozens of derived forms through different affix combinations.

dialects.classification: Filipino is the standardized form of Tagalog. Other Philippine languages (Cebuano, Ilocano, Hiligaynon) are separate languages, not dialects.

encyclopedic.history: Filipino (based on Tagalog) was established as the national language in 1937 and became an official language alongside English in 1987. The Philippines is one of the most English-proficient countries in Asia.

foundations.name: Komisyon sa Wikang Filipino (Commission on the Filipino Language)

name: Finnish

formality.description: Finnish has no meaningful T-V distinction in modern usage. 'Sinä' (you, singular) is universal. The archaic 'Te' (formal you) is essentially never used in software or modern communication. Formality is expressed through vocabulary and tone, not pronouns.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Finnish has no grammatical gender. The third-person pronoun 'hän' is gender-neutral.

professional.label: Ammattimainen (Professional)
professional.description: Standard for software UI and business communication.
professional.prompt: Professional Finnish. Clear, modern language appropriate for software interfaces. Finnish has no formal/informal pronoun distinction — sinä is universal. Avoid overly colloquial expressions.

notes: Finnish is agglutinative with extensive morphology. UI strings tend to be longer than English equivalents.

linguisticChallenges.fifteenCases: Finnish has 15 grammatical cases that modify noun endings. Placeholder variables create severe challenges — the case of the interpolated noun depends on its role in the sentence. '{file} has been deleted' requires the nominative, but 'in {folder}' requires the inessive (-ssa/-ssä).
linguisticChallenges.agglutination: Finnish is heavily agglutinative — 'talossanikin' (in my house too) is one word with four morphemes. MT systems struggle to produce correct multi-suffix forms and sometimes over-generate or under-generate suffixes.
linguisticChallenges.consonantGradation: Consonant gradation changes consonants in stem syllables when suffixes are added (pp→p, tt→t, kk→k, and more complex patterns). Incorrect gradation produces misspellings immediately noticeable to native speakers.
linguisticChallenges.noArticles: Finnish has no articles (no 'the' or 'a'). MT from English sometimes introduces unnecessary demonstratives ('tämä', 'se') to compensate, which sounds unnatural.
linguisticChallenges.possessiveSuffixes: Finnish can express possession through suffixes (-ni my, -si your, -nsa their) instead of separate words. The suffix form is more natural in many contexts — 'taloni' (my house) rather than 'minun talo'. MT tends to use the separate pronoun form, which sounds formal.
linguisticChallenges.longWords: Agglutination produces very long words — 'epäjärjestelmällisyydellisyydestä' (about the quality of being unsystematic) is grammatically valid. While UI text won't hit this extreme, compound words regularly exceed 20 characters, creating layout challenges.

dialects.classification: Standard Finnish based on western dialects. Eastern and western dialect groups differ but are mutually intelligible.

encyclopedic.history: Finnish gained official status alongside Swedish in 1863. Despite geographic proximity, Finnish is not related to Swedish, Norwegian, or any other Indo-European language — its closest major relative is Estonian.

foundations.name: Kotimaisten kielten keskus (Institute for the Languages of Finland)

formality.description: French has a T-V distinction: 'tu' (informal, singular) vs 'vous' (formal or plural). Software UI overwhelmingly uses vous-form for professional contexts. Tu-form is common in consumer/social apps targeting younger demographics. Écriture inclusive (gender-inclusive writing) uses mid-point or raised period notation (e.g., connecté·e).

gender.inclusiveGuidance: French has grammatical gender on nouns, adjectives, and past participles. When the user's gender is unknown, prefer écriture inclusive with the interpunct/middle dot (e.g., "Connecté·e" not "Connecté(e)" or "Connectée"). For plurals, use the inclusive form (e.g., "Utilisateur·rice·s") where readability permits.

formal-vous.label: Vouvoiement (Formal)
formal-vous.description: Standard for professional software, B2B, corporate, and institutional contexts.
formal-vous.prompt: Formal French. Use vous-form (vouvoiement) consistently. Professional, academic register. Avoid colloquialisms.

casual-tu.label: Tutoiement (Casual)
casual-tu.description: For consumer apps, social media, gaming, and startups targeting a casual audience.
casual-tu.prompt: Casual French. Use tu-form (tutoiement). Warm, conversational tone appropriate for consumer apps and social platforms. Contractions and informal expressions are acceptable.

linguisticChallenges.ecritureInclusive: Gender-inclusive French (écriture inclusive) is a live stylistic debate. The interpunct notation (connecté·e·s) is increasingly standard in corporate contexts but some MT models don't produce it. The Académie française opposes it. Style guidance must be clear in the register prompt — defaulting to masculine plural is safe but increasingly dated.
linguisticChallenges.typographicConventions: French typography requires a thin non-breaking space before double punctuation (: ; ? !). This is the single most common error in machine-translated French — straight 'Hello!' should become 'Bonjour !' with a thin-nbsp. Guillemets (« ») also take inner thin-nbsp: « Bonjour ». Most MT systems and even many human translators miss these spaces.
linguisticChallenges.tvConsistency: Mixing tu and vous within the same product is a glaring error. The choice must be consistent across all screens, notifications, error messages, and documentation. MT systems that translate strings independently often produce inconsistent T-V usage.
linguisticChallenges.genderedAgreement: Past participles and adjectives must agree with their subject's gender and number. 'Vous êtes connecté' (masculine) vs 'Vous êtes connectée' (feminine). When user gender is unknown, this forces a choice that has political implications. Infinitive constructions can sometimes avoid the issue: 'Pour se connecter...'
linguisticChallenges.textExpansion: French text is typically 15–20% longer than English, sometimes more. UI layouts must accommodate this expansion. Button labels, navigation items, and tooltips are especially vulnerable to overflow.
linguisticChallenges.falseCognates: French-English false cognates (faux amis) trip up MT regularly — 'actuellement' means 'currently' not 'actually', 'librairie' means 'bookstore' not 'library', 'éventuellement' means 'possibly' not 'eventually'.

dialects.classification: Standard French based on Parisian dialect; significant regional variants in Quebec, Belgium, Switzerland, and West Africa

encyclopedic.history: French was the dominant language of international diplomacy until the 20th century. It remains an official language of 29 countries and numerous international organizations. The Académie française has regulated the language since 1635.

name: Georgian

formality.description: Georgian has a T-V distinction: შენ (shen, informal/singular 'you') vs თქვენ (tkven, formal/plural 'you'). Software UI typically uses თქვენ-form for professional contexts. The distinction is similar to French tu/vous. Georgian also marks politeness through verb morphology and vocabulary choice.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Georgian has no grammatical gender. Third-person pronouns (ის/ისინი) are gender-neutral. No special inclusive conventions are needed.

professional.label: პროფესიონალური (Professional)
professional.description: Standard for software UI, websites, and business communication.
professional.prompt: Professional Georgian. Use თქვენ (tkven) for second-person address. Clear, modern language appropriate for software interfaces. Avoid overly literary or archaic phrasing. Georgian verbs are polypersonal — ensure subject and object agreement is correct.

informal.label: არაფორმალური (Informal)
informal.description: For consumer apps, social media, and casual contexts.
informal.prompt: Informal Georgian. Use შენ (shen) for second-person address. Conversational, friendly tone for consumer apps. Modern vocabulary acceptable.

notes: Georgian uses the Mkhedruli script, which has no uppercase/lowercase distinction in modern usage. The Asomtavruli (uppercase) and Nuskhuri (lowercase) scripts are historical/ecclesiastical. Georgian is the only Kartvelian language with significant digital resources.

linguisticChallenges.polypersonalVerbs: Georgian verbs agree with both subject and object simultaneously (polypersonal agreement). A single verb form encodes who is doing what to whom, making it easy for MT to produce verbs with incorrect agreement markers.
linguisticChallenges.ergativeAlignment: Georgian uses ergative-absolutive case marking in the aorist tense series — the subject of a transitive verb takes ergative case while the object takes nominative. This switches to nominative-accusative in present tense, a split-ergativity pattern that confuses MT systems.
linguisticChallenges.verbScreenes: Georgian has a complex tense-aspect-mood system called 'screeves' (მწკრივი) with 11 distinct forms organized into 3 series. Each series triggers different case marking on arguments. MT systems often produce the wrong screeve.
linguisticChallenges.noArticles: Georgian has no definite or indefinite articles. Definiteness is expressed through context, word order, and demonstratives. MT from English tends to over-translate 'the' and 'a' into unnecessary demonstratives.
linguisticChallenges.postpositions: Georgian primarily uses postpositions rather than prepositions, and many spatial relationships are expressed through case suffixes rather than separate words. The system of 8 cases interacts with postpositions in ways MT frequently mishandles.
linguisticChallenges.compoundWordsAndVerbPrefixes: Georgian uses preverbs (verbal prefixes) that fundamentally change verb meaning and indicate direction of action. The prefix მო- (mo-) vs მი- (mi-) marks motion toward vs away from the speaker — a distinction English doesn't encode in verbs.

formality.description: German has a clear T-V distinction: 'Sie' (formal, always capitalized) vs 'du' (informal). Professional software almost universally uses Sie-form. Consumer tech and social platforms increasingly use du-form ('duzen'). German also has active gender-inclusive writing conventions using the Genderstern (*), colon (:), or Binnen-I.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: German has three grammatical genders (masculine, feminine, neuter). For gender-inclusive language, use the Doppelpunkt (colon) notation: 'Benutzer:innen' (users), 'Mitarbeiter:innen' (employees). The colon is the most widely accepted form in corporate and public communication. Alternatives: Genderstern 'Benutzer*innen' (more progressive), slash 'Benutzer/innen' (older style).

formal-Sie.label: Siezen (Formal)
formal-Sie.description: Standard for professional software, enterprise, B2B, corporate, and institutional contexts.
formal-Sie.prompt: Formal German. Use Sie-form (Siezen) consistently — always capitalized. Professional register. Avoid anglicisms where good German equivalents exist.

casual-du.label: Duzen (Casual)
casual-du.description: For consumer apps, startups, gaming, social media, and products with a casual brand voice.
casual-du.prompt: Casual German. Use du-form (Duzen). Warm, approachable tone for consumer products. Anglicisms acceptable when they're part of standard tech vocabulary (e.g., 'App', 'Download').

linguisticChallenges.compoundNouns: German creates compound nouns by concatenating words without spaces — Datenschutzgrundverordnung (data protection basic regulation), Benutzeroberfläche (user interface). MT systems frequently insert spaces into compounds or fail to form them, producing grammatically incorrect output. These compounds also create challenges for text wrapping and responsive layouts.
linguisticChallenges.nounCapitalization: All German nouns are capitalized, not just proper nouns. Failing to capitalize nouns (e.g., 'benutzer' instead of 'Benutzer') is a conspicuous error. MT models trained on mixed-case data sometimes produce lowercase nouns, especially in lists or after certain punctuation.
linguisticChallenges.genderedAddress: The Genderstern (Benutzer*innen), Doppelpunkt (Benutzer:innen), and Binnen-I (BenutzerInnen) are all in active use for gender-inclusive writing. There is no consensus — the choice is a branding decision with cultural implications. MT models rarely produce any of these forms without explicit prompting.
linguisticChallenges.tvConsistency: German Sie/du distinction is absolute — mixing them is worse than in French because capitalized 'Sie' (formal you) vs lowercase 'sie' (she/they) creates genuine ambiguity. Every string in the product must use the same form consistently.
linguisticChallenges.umlauts: Umlauts (ä, ö, ü) and the Eszett (ß) are essential. Replacing them with ae, oe, ue, ss is acceptable only for legacy systems that can't handle Unicode. MT systems occasionally produce these substitutions, which reads as either archaic or system-constrained.
linguisticChallenges.textExpansion: German text is typically 20–30% longer than English — the longest expansion of any major European language. Compound nouns especially can create extremely long single words. UI layouts must be designed with this in mind from the start.

dialects.classification: Standard German (Hochdeutsch) with significant national variants in Austria (Österreichisches Deutsch) and Switzerland (Schweizer Hochdeutsch)

encyclopedic.history: Standard German was largely codified through Luther's Bible translation (1534) and later standardized by Konrad Duden's dictionary (1880). The 1996 orthographic reform unified spelling across German-speaking countries.

formality.description: Modern Greek has a T-V distinction: εσείς (eseis, formal/plural) vs εσύ (esi, informal/singular). Professional software uses εσείς. Greek uses Δημοτική (Dimotiki, demotic/modern standard) — Καθαρεύουσα (Katharevousa, purist) is archaic.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Greek has three grammatical genders (masculine, feminine, neuter). When gender is unknown, masculine plural is the conventional default.

formal.label: Εσείς (Formal)
formal.description: Standard for professional software and business communication.
formal.prompt: Formal Modern Greek (Δημοτική). Use εσείς for second-person address. Professional register. Modern standard Greek — not Καθαρεύουσα.

informal.label: Εσύ (Informal)
informal.description: For consumer apps, social media, and casual contexts.
informal.prompt: Informal Modern Greek. Use εσύ-form. Conversational tone for consumer products.

linguisticChallenges.polytonic: Modern Greek uses a monotonic accentuation system (single accent mark), but some scholarly and religious texts use polytonic. MT should always produce monotonic unless specifically instructed otherwise.
linguisticChallenges.caseSystem: Greek has four grammatical cases (nominative, genitive, accusative, vocative) that affect articles, nouns, and adjectives. Less complex than Slavic languages but still creates placeholder challenges.
linguisticChallenges.formalAddress: Greek uses εσείς (eseis, formal/plural) vs εσύ (esy, informal). The formal εσείς is standard in software UI.
linguisticChallenges.greeklish: Greeks sometimes write in Latin characters (Greeklish) informally. MT should never produce Greeklish — output must always be in Greek script.

dialects.classification: Standard Modern Greek (Νέα Ελληνικά) based on Demotic Greek. The katharevousa/demotic language question was resolved in favor of Demotic in 1976.

encyclopedic.history: Greek has the longest documented history of any Indo-European language, with records spanning 3,400 years. Modern Greek descends from Koine Greek, the lingua franca of the Hellenistic period.

name: Hebrew

formality.description: Modern Hebrew does not have a T-V pronoun distinction — אתה (ata, masculine) / את (at, feminine) are used regardless of formality. Formality is expressed through vocabulary, sentence structure, and avoidance of slang. Hebrew verbs conjugate for gender in second and third person.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Hebrew has grammatical gender on verbs, adjectives, and pronouns — there is no gender-neutral second person. For UI text addressing unknown users, prefer infinitive or imperative constructions that avoid gendered verb forms. Some modern Hebrew uses slash notation (e.g., 'מחובר/ת'). The slash form is increasingly accepted in digital contexts.

professional.label: מקצועי (Professional)
professional.description: Standard for software UI and business communication.
professional.prompt: Professional Modern Hebrew. RTL layout. Clear, modern vocabulary.

formal.label: רשמי (Formal)
formal.description: For government, legal, and official contexts.
formal.prompt: Formal Hebrew. Elevated register for government, legal, and official communications. Precise vocabulary, complex sentence structures.

notes: The deprecated ISO 639-1 code 'iw' is registered as an alias. Java and older systems still use 'iw'.

linguisticChallenges.bidirectionalText: Hebrew is RTL like Arabic, creating the same bidi challenges with embedded LTR content (variables, URLs, numbers, code). Placeholder strings like '{count} קבצות' require careful bidi handling to prevent visual reordering.
linguisticChallenges.genderedVerbs: Hebrew verbs conjugate for gender in second and third person — there is no gender-neutral 'you did'. 'עשית' (you did, masculine) vs 'עשית' (you did, feminine — same consonants but different pronunciation). For written UI, this is a real challenge. The slash form (עשית/עשית) or infinitive constructions ('לעשות') are common workarounds.
linguisticChallenges.rootPatternMorphology: Like Arabic, Hebrew uses root-pattern morphology where three-consonant roots generate word families through vowel patterns. K-T-B → כתב (wrote), כתיבה (writing), מכתב (letter), כתובת (address).
linguisticChallenges.definiteness: Hebrew marks definiteness with the prefix ה- (ha-). When a noun is definite, its adjective must also take the definite prefix: 'הקובץ הגדול' (the big file) not 'הקובץ גדול'. MT sometimes misses adjective definiteness agreement.
linguisticChallenges.nikkud: Hebrew vowels (nikkud/ניקוד) are usually omitted in modern text, creating ambiguity — 'דבר' could be 'davar' (thing), 'diber' (spoke), or 'dever' (plague). MT must disambiguate from context without producing unnecessary nikkud (which looks pedagogical in modern text).
linguisticChallenges.dualNumber: Hebrew has a dual number (-ayim suffix) used primarily for paired body parts and time units: 'שעתיים' (two hours), 'עיניים' (two eyes). This is less productive than Arabic's dual but must be handled correctly.

dialects.classification: Modern Hebrew (Ivrit) is remarkably uniform due to its revival as a spoken language in the 20th century. No significant dialectal variation in software contexts.

encyclopedic.history: Hebrew was revived as a spoken language in the late 19th century by Eliezer Ben-Yehuda after centuries of use primarily as a liturgical and literary language. It is the only successfully revived dead language in history.

foundations.name: Academy of the Hebrew Language (האקדמיה ללשון העברית)

name: Hindi

formality.description: Hindi has a three-tier pronoun system: आप (āp, formal/respectful), तुम (tum, familiar), and तू (tū, intimate/condescending). Software UI uses आप universally. Hindi also has a code-mixing dimension: modern Indian tech communication naturally mixes Hindi with English (Hinglish). Pure शुद्ध हिन्दी (shuddh Hindi, 'pure Hindi') without English loanwords reads as formal/literary and is unusual in tech contexts.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Hindi verbs and adjectives agree in gender. When addressing users of unknown gender, prefer restructuring sentences to avoid gender agreement where possible (e.g., using infinitive or passive constructions). When agreement is unavoidable, use masculine as the socially conventional unmarked form, but note this is a pragmatic default, not an endorsement — style guides vary.

professional-hinglish.label: प्रोफेशनल (Professional Hinglish)
professional-hinglish.description: Standard for software UI and tech products in India. Hindi structure with English for technical and UI terms.
professional-hinglish.prompt: Professional Hindi with natural Hindi-English code-mixing (Hinglish). Use Hindi sentence structure but keep technical terms, UI labels, and industry-standard vocabulary in English — this matches how Indian professionals actually use digital products. Use आप (āp) for second person. Devanagari script for Hindi words, Latin for English terms.

shuddh-formal.label: शुद्ध हिन्दी (Pure Hindi, Formal)
shuddh-formal.description: For government, academic, or cultural contexts where pure Hindi without English loanwords is preferred.
shuddh-formal.prompt: Formal शुद्ध हिन्दी (pure Hindi). Minimize English loanwords — use Hindi equivalents where they exist (e.g., 'संगणक' for computer, 'जालस्थल' for website). Formal आप-form. Appropriate for government, educational, and cultural institutions.

casual-hinglish.label: कैज़ुअल (Casual Hinglish)
casual-hinglish.description: For social media, gaming, and youth-oriented products. Heavy code-mixing.
casual-hinglish.prompt: Casual Hinglish. Heavy Hindi-English code-mixing with conversational tone. Use तुम (tum) for second person. Colloquial expressions and internet-influenced language acceptable. For social media, gaming, and youth-oriented apps.

notes: Previous default ('Minimize English loanwords') was incorrect for modern Indian tech context. Changed to Hinglish professional as default, which matches actual usage patterns.

linguisticChallenges.codeSwitchingCalibration: The biggest Hindi translation challenge is calibrating the Hindi-English mix. Pure Hindi (शुद्ध हिन्दी) sounds overly formal or government-like in tech contexts. Too much English sounds like the translator gave up. The right balance varies by audience — urban tech users expect more English, while government/education contexts expect more Hindi.
linguisticChallenges.genderAgreement: Hindi verbs, adjectives, and postpositions agree in gender. The default masculine can create awkward output when the subject is feminine or unknown. Restructuring sentences to use infinitive/passive constructions avoids the problem but can sound stilted.
linguisticChallenges.postpositions: Hindi uses postpositions (में in, पर on, से from) instead of prepositions. These attach after the noun, which affects variable/placeholder positioning — English 'in {city}' becomes '{city} में'. MT systems sometimes place postpositions before the noun by calquing English structure.
linguisticChallenges.devanagariConjuncts: Devanagari script uses conjunct consonants (संयुक्त अक्षर) where consonant clusters merge visually (e.g., क + र = क्र). Some MT outputs produce incorrect conjuncts or fail to form them, creating misspelled text that may render differently across fonts.
linguisticChallenges.sovWordOrder: Hindi is SOV (Subject-Object-Verb) with relatively free word order. Literal SVO translation from English sounds unnatural. Variables in strings must account for Hindi word order — 'You have {count} messages' → 'आपके पास {count} संदेश हैं'.
linguisticChallenges.hindiUrduContinuum: Hindi and Urdu are mutually intelligible in spoken form but use different scripts (Devanagari vs Nastaliq) and draw vocabulary from different sources (Sanskrit vs Persian/Arabic). MT models sometimes produce Urdu-influenced vocabulary in Hindi output, especially for formal/literary registers.

dialects.classification: Standard Hindi (Modern Standard Hindi) based on Khariboli dialect; regional variants include Braj, Awadhi, Bhojpuri

encyclopedic.history: Hindi was standardized during the 19th century and adopted as one of India's two official languages (alongside English) in 1950. The Hindi Belt in northern India is the language's heartland, but Hindi serves as a lingua franca across much of India.

formality.description: Hungarian has a T-V distinction: Ön (formal, third-person construction) vs te (informal). Professional software uses Ön-form. Hungarian also has 'maga' (slightly less formal than Ön, but more distant than te), though it's rarely used in software.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Hungarian has no grammatical gender. Third-person pronoun 'ő' is gender-neutral.

formal-on.label: Ön (Formal)
formal-on.description: Standard for professional software and business communication.
formal-on.prompt: Formal Hungarian. Use Ön-form (Önözés) for second-person address. Professional register for software interfaces.

informal-te.label: Te (Informal)
informal-te.description: For consumer apps, social media, and casual contexts.
informal-te.prompt: Informal Hungarian. Use te-form (Tegezés). Conversational tone for consumer products.

linguisticChallenges.agglutination: Hungarian is heavily agglutinative with 18 grammatical cases. A single noun can accumulate case suffix, possessive suffix, and plural marker: 'házaimban' (in my houses). MT must correctly sequence and combine these suffixes.
linguisticChallenges.definiteConjugation: Hungarian has two complete verb conjugation paradigms — definite and indefinite — based on whether the direct object is definite. 'Látom a fájlt' (I see THE file, definite) vs 'Látok egy fájlt' (I see A file, indefinite). Choosing the wrong conjugation is a glaring error. MT systems frequently confuse these.
linguisticChallenges.vowelHarmony: Like Turkish, Hungarian has strict vowel harmony (front/back). Suffixes change vowels to match their stem — '-ban/-ben' (in), '-nak/-nek' (for). Breaking vowel harmony is immediately wrong.
linguisticChallenges.nameOrderReversed: Hungarian uses Eastern name order (family name first): 'Kovács János' not 'János Kovács'. This affects placeholder strings — '{firstName} {lastName}' must be reversed. MT from English often preserves Western name order.
linguisticChallenges.postpositions: Hungarian uses postpositions instead of prepositions — 'az asztal alatt' (the table under = under the table). Variable positioning in strings must account for this.
linguisticChallenges.noGrammaticalGender: Like Turkish and Finnish, Hungarian has no grammatical gender — 'ő' is gender-neutral he/she. This simplifies inclusive writing but complicates translation to gendered languages.

dialects.classification: Standard Hungarian based on the central (Budapest) dialect. Dialects differ slightly in vocabulary and pronunciation but are mutually intelligible.

encyclopedic.history: Hungarian is a Uralic language, unrelated to its Slavic and Germanic neighbors. The closest relatives are the Ob-Ugric languages (Khanty and Mansi) spoken in western Siberia. Written records date to 1055 CE.

name: Indonesian

formality.description: Indonesian does not have a T-V pronoun distinction in the European sense. 'Anda' (formal you) vs 'kamu' (informal you) exists but software UI generally uses 'Anda' or avoids direct address entirely using passive constructions. Formality is expressed through vocabulary choice and use of formal prefixes/suffixes.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Indonesian has no grammatical gender. Pronouns are gender-neutral.

professional.label: Profesional (Professional)
professional.description: Standard for software UI and business communication.
professional.prompt: Professional Indonesian (Bahasa Indonesia). Use Anda (capitalized) for second-person address, or prefer passive/impersonal constructions to avoid direct address. Clear, modern language appropriate for software interfaces.

notes: Indonesian (Bahasa Indonesia) and Malay (Bahasa Melayu) are mutually intelligible but have distinct standard forms. See ms.json for Malay.

linguisticChallenges.noConjugation: Indonesian has no verb conjugation, no tense markers, and no noun declension. Time is expressed through context and adverbs. MT from heavily inflected languages sometimes produces redundant temporal markers.
linguisticChallenges.affixSystem: Indonesian uses a complex prefix/suffix system (me-/ber-/pe-/ke-/-kan/-an/-i) to modify word meaning and grammatical role. The base word 'tulis' (write) → menulis (to write), penulis (writer), tulisan (writing), ditulis (was written).
linguisticChallenges.indonesianVsMalay: Indonesian and Malay are closely related but differ in vocabulary and some spelling conventions. MT must not mix them.

dialects.classification: Standard Indonesian (Bahasa Indonesia) based on Riau Malay. Despite Indonesia's enormous linguistic diversity (700+ languages), Indonesian is relatively uniform.

encyclopedic.history: Indonesian was chosen as the national language at independence (1945) despite being the native language of only ~7% of the population, because of its role as a trade lingua franca and its perceived neutrality among ethnic groups.

formality.description: Italian has a T-V distinction: 'tu' (informal) vs 'Lei' (formal, third-person singular, always capitalized). 'Voi' (second-person plural used as formal singular) is archaic in most regions but still used in some southern dialects. Professional software uses Lei-form.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Italian has grammatical gender on nouns, adjectives, and past participles. The schwa (ə) for gender-neutral endings is used in progressive contexts but is not widely adopted in professional software. Prefer restructuring sentences to avoid gendered forms, or use both forms with slash (e.g., 'Connesso/a') when brevity is needed.

formal-Lei.label: Lei (Formal)
formal-Lei.description: Standard for professional software, enterprise, B2B, and institutional contexts.
formal-Lei.prompt: Formal Italian. Use Lei-form consistently (always capitalized). Professional register. Avoid colloquialisms and regional expressions.

informal-tu.label: Tu (Informal)
informal-tu.description: For consumer apps, social media, gaming, and casual brand voices.
informal-tu.prompt: Informal Italian. Use tu-form. Warm, conversational tone appropriate for consumer products. Colloquial expressions acceptable when natural.

linguisticChallenges.leiCapitalization: The formal pronoun 'Lei' (you, formal) must be capitalized in writing to distinguish it from 'lei' (she). MT systems frequently produce lowercase 'lei' in formal contexts, which changes the meaning from formal address to third-person reference. This is one of the most common MT errors in Italian.
linguisticChallenges.congiuntivoMood: Italian has a productive subjunctive (congiuntivo) used after verbs of belief, doubt, wish, and in subordinate clauses. Its decline in spoken Italian leads MT models to under-produce it. In formal written text, missing congiuntivo ('Credo che *è' instead of 'Credo che sia') is a conspicuous grammatical error.
linguisticChallenges.doubleConsonants: Italian distinguishes single from double consonants in both pronunciation and meaning — 'pala' (shovel) vs 'palla' (ball), 'caro' (dear) vs 'carro' (cart). MT models sometimes drop or double consonants incorrectly, producing misspellings or meaning changes.
linguisticChallenges.articlesWithProperNouns: Italian uses definite articles before possessives ('il mio account'), days of the week ('il lunedì'), and in some regional usage, before first names ('la Maria' — Northern). MT must follow standard usage for each context.
linguisticChallenges.elisionAndTruncation: Italian requires elision (l'utente, not 'lo utente') and truncation (un amico, not 'uno amico') following specific phonological rules. MT systems occasionally fail to apply these, producing stilted output that no native speaker would write.
linguisticChallenges.pronominalVerbs: Italian pronominal verbs (farcela, cavarsela, andarsene) are semantically opaque and highly idiomatic. They combine a verb with multiple clitics and change meaning unpredictably. MT frequently translates them literally component-by-component, losing the intended meaning entirely.

dialects.classification: Standard Italian based on Tuscan/Florentine dialect, codified during the Renaissance. Regional dialects exist but are separate languages by linguistic criteria; they are not used in software.

encyclopedic.history: Modern Standard Italian is based on the literary Tuscan of Dante, Petrarch, and Boccaccio. It became the national language after Italian unification in 1861, though regional dialects remained dominant in daily speech well into the 20th century.

name: Japanese

formality.description: Japanese has a complex honorific system (keigo) with three layers: 丁寧語 (polite language), 尊敬語 (respectful language, elevates the listener), and 謙譲語 (humble language, lowers the speaker). Software UI typically uses です/ます (polite form) for body text, with plain form (する/だ) for short UI elements like button labels and navigation. Full keigo is reserved for customer service and formal business communication.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Japanese has gendered first-person pronouns (僕 boku/俺 ore = masculine, あたし atashi = feminine). Use 私 (watashi) as the neutral default in UI text. Avoid gendered sentence-final particles (わ wa, ぞ zo) unless the product has a specific persona.

polite.label: です/ます (Polite)
polite.description: Standard for software UI. Polite and natural. Button labels and short elements can use plain form.
polite.prompt: Polite Japanese (です/ます form) for body text and descriptions. For short UI elements like button labels, menu items, and navigation, plain form (する) is natural and preferred. Use appropriate kanji density — avoid overly literary kanji compounds that would require furigana. Use 私 (watashi) for first-person if needed. Neutral, professional tone.

formal-keigo.label: 敬語 (Honorific Keigo)
formal-keigo.description: For customer service, formal business, government, or luxury brand interfaces.
formal-keigo.prompt: Formal Japanese with full keigo (敬語). Use 丁寧語 (polite language) as baseline, 尊敬語 (respectful) when referencing user actions, 謙譲語 (humble) when the system refers to its own actions. Appropriate for customer service portals, banking, government, and luxury brands.

casual.label: タメ口 (Casual)
casual.description: For gaming, social media, youth-oriented apps. Plain form throughout.
casual.prompt: Casual Japanese (タメ口). Plain form (だ/する) throughout. Natural conversational tone appropriate for gaming, social media, and apps targeting younger demographics. Light use of カタカナ for emphasis is acceptable.

notes: Japanese uses three scripts simultaneously: kanji (漢字), hiragana (ひらがな), and katakana (カタカナ). The balance between them affects tone — more kanji reads as more formal, more hiragana reads as softer/simpler.

linguisticChallenges.scriptMixing: Japanese uses three scripts simultaneously (kanji, hiragana, katakana). The balance affects register — more kanji reads as formal/literary, more hiragana as softer/childlike, and katakana for foreign loanwords and emphasis. MT systems frequently get the kanji density wrong for the target register.
linguisticChallenges.honorificVerbEndings: Verb endings encode the entire politeness level. A single wrong ending (e.g., する instead of します) immediately shifts the register. MT must be consistent throughout a passage — mixing です/ます with plain form in the same paragraph is a visible error.
linguisticChallenges.counters: Japanese uses specific counter suffixes (助数詞) for different noun categories — 枚 (flat objects), 本 (cylindrical), 匹 (small animals), 台 (machines/vehicles). Using the generic counter 個 for everything sounds unnatural. MT frequently falls back to 個 instead of the correct counter.
linguisticChallenges.subjectOmission: Japanese regularly omits subjects and objects when contextually obvious. Over-specifying them (common in literal MT from English) sounds robotic and unnatural. Good translation means knowing what to leave unsaid.
linguisticChallenges.sentenceFinalParticles: Particles like ね (seeking agreement), よ (asserting), わ (softening, gendered) change nuance. Avoid gendered particles (わ, ぞ) in neutral UI text. MT systems sometimes insert them inappropriately.
linguisticChallenges.katakanaLoanwords: Technical terms are often written in katakana (e.g., サーバー server, ダウンロード download). Using kanji equivalents for these reads as overly formal or archaic. Conversely, writing common Japanese words in katakana implies emphasis or irony.

dialects.classification: Standard Japanese (標準語) based on Tokyo dialect; regional dialects exist but are not used in software

encyclopedic.history: Modern Standard Japanese was codified during the Meiji era. The writing system combines Chinese-origin kanji with two indigenous syllabaries (hiragana and katakana), creating unique challenges for NLP and text processing.

name: Klingon

formality.description: Klingon has a deliberately harsh register by design (created by linguist Marc Okrand for Star Trek). There is no polite/formal distinction — Klingons don't do polite. The language has OVS word order and case-sensitive romanization (D ≠ d, Q ≠ q, S ≠ s, I ≠ i).

standard.label: Standard Klingon
standard.description: Direct, imperative Klingon following Marc Okrand's canon vocabulary.
standard.prompt: Standard Klingon (tlhIngan Hol). Use only vocabulary from Marc Okrand's canonical sources (The Klingon Dictionary, Klingon for the Galactic Traveler, etc.). Case-sensitive romanization is critical: D (retroflex), Q (uvular), S (retroflex), I (vowel) are distinct phonemes. OVS word order. Direct, imperative tone — Klingon does not have a polite register.

warrior.label: Warrior Register
warrior.description: Aggressive, commanding tone with military vocabulary. For gaming and themed applications.
warrior.prompt: Warrior-register Klingon. Aggressive, commanding tone. Prefer military and battle metaphors. Short, imperative sentences. batlh (honor) and may' (battle) vocabulary.

notes: Klingon pIqaD script requires a PUA-compatible font (U+F8D0–F8FF). The script converter transforms romanized Klingon to pIqaD.

name: Korean

formality.description: Korean has seven speech levels (존댓말 jondaenmal = polite / 반말 banmal = casual). The three relevant to software are: 합쇼체 (hapsyo-che, highest formality — news anchors, military, government), 해요체 (haeyo-che, polite — standard for most software UI, natural and respectful), and 해체 (hae-che, casual — social media, gaming). Most Korean software uses 해요체.

polite-haeyo.label: 해요체 (Polite)
polite-haeyo.description: Standard for software UI, websites, and professional communication. Natural and respectful without being stiff.
polite-haeyo.prompt: Polite Korean (해요체). Standard register for software UI and professional communication. Natural and respectful without being stiff or overly deferential. Sentence endings use -요 forms. This is what users expect from apps, websites, and most digital products.

formal-hapsyo.label: 합쇼체 (Formal)
formal-hapsyo.description: For government, legal, corporate reports, or official announcements. Very formal.
formal-hapsyo.prompt: Formal Korean (합쇼체). Highest politeness level with -ㅂ니다/-습니다 endings. For government portals, legal text, corporate reports, and official announcements. This register is intentionally stiff and authoritative.

casual-hae.label: 해체 (Casual)
casual-hae.description: For social media, chat apps, gaming, and youth-oriented products.
casual-hae.prompt: Casual Korean (해체/반말). Plain form without politeness markers. For social media, messaging apps, gaming, and products targeting younger demographics. Natural conversational tone.

notes: The previous default (합쇼체) was too formal for software UI. Changed to 해요체, which is the actual industry standard for Korean digital products.

linguisticChallenges.speechLevelConsistency: Korean speech levels (존댓말 vs 반말) must be consistent throughout a passage. Mixing 해요체 endings with 합쇼체 endings in the same screen is immediately noticeable and reads as incoherent. MT systems frequently mix levels within a single response.
linguisticChallenges.sovWordOrder: Korean is strictly SOV (Subject-Object-Verb). Variables and placeholders in translation strings must account for this — English '{name} uploaded {file}' becomes '{name}님이 {file}을(를) 업로드했습니다' with the verb at the end and particles attached to nouns.
linguisticChallenges.agglutinativeParticles: Grammatical particles (은/는, 이/가, 을/를) attach directly to nouns and vary based on whether the preceding syllable ends in a consonant. Placeholder variables create challenges because the correct particle depends on the runtime value — use 이(가) or 을(를) notation to cover both cases.
linguisticChallenges.honorificVocabulary: Korean has entirely different words for some concepts depending on formality — 밥 (bap, rice/meal, casual) vs 식사 (siksa, meal, formal), 이름 (ireum, name) vs 성함 (seongham, name, honorific). Using the wrong register word is more jarring than using the wrong speech level.
linguisticChallenges.spacingRules: Korean has specific word-spacing (띄어쓰기) conventions that differ from English. Particles attach to the preceding noun without a space, but compound words follow complex spacing rules that native speakers themselves often debate.
linguisticChallenges.dualNumberSystems: Korean uses two number systems: native Korean (하나, 둘, 셋) for counting and age, and Sino-Korean (일, 이, 삼) for dates, phone numbers, and large quantities. Using the wrong system sounds unnatural.

dialects.classification: Standard Korean (표준어) based on Seoul dialect; North Korean standard (문화어) differs in vocabulary and some pronunciation

encyclopedic.history: Hangul was created in 1443 by King Sejong as a systematic phonetic alphabet. Its logical design (consonant shapes mirror tongue position) makes it one of the most scientifically designed writing systems.

name: Kryptonian

formality.description: Constructed language or stylistic variant — no formality system. Tone is defined entirely by the register preset.

standard.label: Standard Kryptonian
standard.description: Formal, dignified Kryptonian voice inspired by Kal-El's heritage.
standard.prompt: Kryptonian register. Translate English into a dignified, slightly formal style inspired by Kryptonian culture from DC Comics. Use elevated vocabulary, declarative sentences, and references to concepts like hope, justice, and legacy where contextually appropriate. The text will be rendered in Kryptonian glyphs via font.

notes: Kryptonian 'script' is a 1:1 Latin cipher rendered via custom PUA font (U+E100–E119). The converter is font-based, not linguistic.

formality.description: Malay formality is similar to Indonesian — 'Anda' (formal you) is used in writing, while spoken Malay uses 'awak' or 'kamu'. Software UI uses 'Anda'. Malaysian Malay has more English loanwords than Indonesian.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Malay has no grammatical gender.

professional.label: Profesional (Professional)
professional.description: Standard for software UI targeting Malaysia, Brunei, and Singapore Malay-speakers.
professional.prompt: Professional Malay (Bahasa Melayu). Use Anda for second-person address. Malaysian standard — English loanwords for tech terms are natural and expected. Clear, modern language.

linguisticChallenges.malayVsIndonesian: Malay and Indonesian are closely related but differ in vocabulary — 'kereta' (MY, car) vs 'mobil' (ID, car), 'telefon bimbit' (MY, mobile) vs 'telepon genggam' (ID, mobile). MT must target the correct variety.
linguisticChallenges.noConjugation: Like Indonesian, Malay has no verb conjugation or grammatical gender. This simplifies some aspects of translation but means context carries more weight.
linguisticChallenges.affixSystem: Malay shares the same affix system as Indonesian but some prefix forms differ in usage and productivity.

dialects.classification: Standard Malay (Bahasa Melayu) based on the Johor-Riau dialect. Identical to Indonesian at the structural level but divergent in vocabulary.

encyclopedic.history: Malay has been a lingua franca of maritime Southeast Asia for centuries. It became the national language of Malaysia (as Bahasa Melayu/Bahasa Malaysia) at independence in 1957.

foundations.name: Dewan Bahasa dan Pustaka (Institute of Language and Literature)

name: Mexican Spanish

formality.description: Mexican Spanish uses tú (informal) and usted (formal). Vos is not used in Mexico. The tone is generally warmer and more direct than neutral Latin American Spanish. Mexico-specific vocabulary differs from other Latin American countries.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Same rules as base Spanish. The 'x' and 'e' inclusive endings are used in Mexican progressive contexts but not yet standard.

professional-tu.label: Tú (Professional, warm)
professional-tu.description: Standard for Mexican software and apps. Tú-form with professional vocabulary but warm, approachable tone.
professional-tu.prompt: Mexican Spanish. Use tú-form — warm, direct tone that is standard for Mexican apps and websites. Mexico-specific vocabulary where it differs from other Latin American varieties. Professional but approachable.

formal-usted.label: Usted (Formal)
formal-usted.description: For government, banking, legal, and enterprise contexts in Mexico.
formal-usted.prompt: Formal Mexican Spanish. Use usted-form. Elevated register for institutional contexts. Mexico-specific vocabulary.

notes: Mexico has the largest Spanish-speaking population worldwide. Mexican Spanish default uses tú-form, which is warmer than the neutral Latin American 'es' default (usted).

linguisticChallenges.mexicanVocabulary: Mexican Spanish has distinct vocabulary from both Peninsular and neutral Latin American Spanish — 'computadora' (computer), 'platicar' (to chat, vs 'hablar/conversar'), 'chido' (cool, casual). MT must produce Mexican-specific terms, not generic LatAm or Peninsular vocabulary.
linguisticChallenges.nahuatlLoanwords: Mexican Spanish includes Nahuatl-origin words not found in other varieties — 'chocolate', 'tomate', 'aguacate' (avocado) are universal, but 'chamaco' (kid), 'mitote' (commotion) are specifically Mexican.
linguisticChallenges.ustedUsage: Mexico uses 'usted' more broadly than many other Latin American countries — it can indicate respect rather than social distance. The tú/usted threshold is different from Argentine or Chilean Spanish.

dialects.classification: Mexican Spanish is relatively uniform in its standard form. Regional differences exist (norteño, costeño, yucateco) but are not relevant for software.

encyclopedic.history: Mexico has the largest Spanish-speaking population in the world. Mexican Spanish has been influenced by Nahuatl and other indigenous languages, creating unique vocabulary and expressions.

name: Norwegian Bokmål

formality.description: Norwegian Bokmål historically had a T-V distinction (De/du) but modern usage has almost completely abandoned 'De' (formal). Software universally uses 'du'. Formality in Norwegian is expressed through vocabulary and tone rather than pronoun choice.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Bokmål has three grammatical genders (masculine, feminine, neuter). For inclusive language referring to people, Norwegian increasingly uses gender-neutral constructions. The pronoun 'hen' is gaining acceptance as a gender-neutral third-person singular alternative to 'han/hun'.

professional.label: Profesjonell (Professional)
professional.description: Standard for software UI and business communication. Modern, clear Bokmål.
professional.prompt: Professional Norwegian Bokmål. Use du-form (De-form is archaic). Modern, clear language appropriate for software interfaces. Bokmål orthography — not Nynorsk.

notes: This fixes the nb/no mismatch. The 'no' code is registered as an alias pointing here. The Nordic preset now correctly uses 'nb'. Norway also has Nynorsk (code 'nn') which is a separate written standard — not yet supported as a language card.

linguisticChallenges.bokmålVsNynorsk: Norway has two official written standards: Bokmål (based on Dano-Norwegian) and Nynorsk (based on rural dialects). MT must target the correct one — they are different enough that mixing them is immediately noticeable. 'nb' targets Bokmål.
linguisticChallenges.threeGenders: Norwegian Bokmål can use either two genders (common/neuter like Danish) or three genders (masculine/feminine/neuter). The three-gender system is more colloquial and increasingly common. MT should be consistent about which system it uses.
linguisticChallenges.compoundNouns: Like other Scandinavian languages, Norwegian forms compounds by concatenation with linking elements.
linguisticChallenges.scandinavianMixing: MT models have particular difficulty distinguishing Norwegian from Danish due to their extreme similarity in written form. Vocabulary differences (e.g., 'snakke' NO vs 'tale' DA for 'speak') must be correct.

dialects.classification: Bokmål and Nynorsk are both official written standards. Bokmål is used by ~85-90% of the population. Spoken dialects vary enormously and have high social prestige.

encyclopedic.history: Bokmål evolved from Dano-Norwegian, the written language used during Norway's union with Denmark (1380–1814). Nynorsk was created by Ivar Aasen in the 19th century as a 'purer' Norwegian based on rural dialects.

name: Persian

formality.description: Persian has a T-V distinction: شما (shomā, formal/plural) vs تو (to, informal/singular). Professional software uses شما-form. Persian also uses elaborate courtesy phrases (تعارف ta'ārof) in formal contexts, which are generally omitted in software UI.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Persian has no grammatical gender. Third-person pronouns (او) are gender-neutral.

formal-shoma.label: شما (Formal)
formal-shoma.description: Standard for professional software and business communication.
formal-shoma.prompt: Formal Persian. Use شما (shomā) for second-person address. Professional register. RTL layout. Modern standard Farsi — avoid excessive تعارف (courtesy phrases). Clear, direct phrasing for software interfaces.

informal-to.label: تو (Informal)
informal-to.description: For consumer apps, social media, and casual contexts.
informal-to.prompt: Informal Persian. Use تو (to) form. Conversational tone for consumer products. RTL layout.

notes: Persian uses Arabic script (modified) but is not Arabic. The script code 'Arab' is correct per ISO 15924.

linguisticChallenges.rtlWithArabicScript: Persian uses a modified Arabic script (with 4 additional letters: پ چ ژ گ) and is RTL. Bidi handling challenges are identical to Arabic. MT must produce correct Persian characters, not Arabic equivalents.
linguisticChallenges.ezafe: Persian uses the ezafe construction (a short unstressed -e/-ye linking nouns to their modifiers) that is usually not written: 'کتاب ِ بزرگ' (ketāb-e bozorg, big book). MT must understand this implicit link even though it's invisible in text.
