linguisticChallenges.formalVsColloquial: Written Persian (فارسی نوشتاری) differs significantly from spoken Persian (فارسی محاوره‌ای). Written uses 'است' (is), spoken uses '-ه'. MT should produce written register for software but spoken register for conversational UI.
linguisticChallenges.persianVsArabicLoanwords: Persian borrows extensively from Arabic but modifies pronunciation and sometimes meaning. MT models sometimes produce Arabic-style morphology instead of the Persianized forms.
linguisticChallenges.zwnj: Persian uses the Zero-Width Non-Joiner (ZWNJ, U+200C) extensively to prevent letters from connecting in compound words: 'می‌خواهم' (I want) — the می and خواهم should be visually separate but connected to the word. Missing ZWNJs make text look wrong.

dialects.classification: Western Persian (Farsi, Iran), Dari (Afghanistan), and Tajik (Tajikistan, Cyrillic script) are the three standard varieties. Farsi and Dari are mutually intelligible; Tajik uses Cyrillic.

encyclopedic.history: Persian is one of the oldest continuously spoken languages, with literary records dating to 500 BCE (Old Persian inscriptions at Persepolis). Modern Persian adopted the Arabic script after the Islamic conquest of the 7th century.

foundations.name: Academy of Persian Language and Literature

name: Pirate English

formality.description: Constructed language or stylistic variant — no formality system. Tone is defined entirely by the register preset.

standard.label: Standard Pirate
standard.description: Classic pirate English with nautical metaphors and Arr vocabulary.
standard.prompt: Pirate English. Translate into stereotypical pirate dialect with nautical vocabulary, 'ye' for 'you', 'me' for 'my', 'Arr' interjections, and seafaring metaphors. Keep UI meaning clear — the joke should enhance, not obscure, the interface. Error messages should still be understandable.

full-send.label: Full Send Pirate
full-send.description: Maximum pirate. Every sentence gets the full treatment. For themed apps and Talk Like a Pirate Day.
full-send.prompt: Maximum pirate English. Every response gets the full treatment — nautical slang, sea shanty rhythm, treasure metaphors, weather references. 'Loading' becomes 'Haulin' anchor', 'Error' becomes 'Blimey!'. Fun trumps clarity.

notes: Register-only conlang — English with pirate style transformation. No script conversion needed.

name: Plains Cree

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Languages in this family utilize an animacy distinction (animate vs inanimate noun classes) rather than grammatical gender.

formality.description: Plains Cree does not have a European-style T-V distinction. Formality is expressed through the use of respectful particles and verb forms. The language has a complex polysynthetic morphology — a single word can encode what requires an entire sentence in English. SRO (Standard Roman Orthography) is the working script; Syllabics (ᓀᐦᐃᔭᐍᐏᐣ) is the display script.

variables.guidance: Variables are marked with braces `{count}`. Since Cree relies heavily on affixation, suffixes must attach directly to placeholders. Use a hyphen to separate the variable from grammatical affixes (e.g., `{count}-ak` or `{count}-a` for plurals).

standard.label: Standard (SRO)
standard.description: Standard Plains Cree in SRO orthography. Clear, accessible language appropriate for educational and community software.
standard.prompt: Standard Plains Cree (nêhiyawêwin) in SRO (Standard Roman Orthography). Use established vocabulary from published Cree dictionaries. When no standard term exists for a technical concept, keep the English term rather than inventing neologisms. Syllabics conversion is handled as a post-processing step.

notes: Low-resource language under active development. Translation quality requires llm-coached method with Cree-specific coaching data. See OCAP principles documentation for community engagement guidelines.

linguisticChallenges.polysynthesis: Cree is highly polysynthetic. A single verb can incorporate subject/object pronouns, instrumentals, locations, and actions. Translate phrases conceptually rather than word-for-word.
linguisticChallenges.animacy: Verb conjugation changes completely based on whether the subject/object nouns are animate or inanimate. Ensure grammatical agreement.
linguisticChallenges.neologisms: Avoid literal translations of modern software concepts. Maintain English loanwords for modern UI elements when no community-accepted Cree term exists.

dialects.classification: Plains Cree (y-dialect)

encyclopedic.history: Plains Cree is the most widely spoken Algonquian language in Canada. Historically, it functioned as a regional trade lingua franca across the Canadian plains.

name: Polish

formality.description: Polish has a T-V distinction using Pan/Pani (Sir/Madam, formal) vs ty (informal). The formal forms are third-person constructions: 'Czy Pan chce...' (Does Sir want...) rather than a pronoun switch. Professional software uses Pan/Pani form, which inherently references gender. Some modern Polish software avoids this by using infinitive constructions or impersonal forms.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Polish has grammatical gender and the formal address (Pan/Pani) is inherently gendered. For gender-neutral UI, prefer impersonal constructions (e.g., 'Proszę wybrać...' — 'Please choose...') or infinitive forms that avoid the Pan/Pani choice. The slash form 'Pan/Pani' can be used when direct address is necessary.

formal-pan.label: Pan/Pani (Formal)
formal-pan.description: Standard for professional software, enterprise, and institutional contexts.
formal-pan.prompt: Formal Polish. Use Pan/Pani form for direct address. Professional register appropriate for software interfaces.

informal-ty.label: Ty (Informal)
informal-ty.description: For consumer apps, social media, gaming, and youth-oriented products.
informal-ty.prompt: Informal Polish. Use ty-form. Conversational, friendly tone for consumer products and social platforms. Colloquial expressions acceptable.

notes: Polish formal address (Pan/Pani) is unique in that it's inherently gendered. This creates a practical challenge for UI text addressing unknown users.

linguisticChallenges.genderedFormalAddress: Polish is unique among European languages in that formal address (Pan/Pani) is inherently gendered — there is no gender-neutral formal pronoun. Software UI addressing unknown users must either use impersonal constructions ('Proszę wybrać...' — Please choose), infinitives, or the Pan/Pani slash notation. MT models rarely handle this automatically.
linguisticChallenges.complexPluralSystem: Polish has four plural categories (one, few, many, other) with complex rules: 1 is 'one', 2-4 use 'few' (dwa pliki), 5-21 use 'many' (pięć plików), then 22-24 cycle back to 'few'. This cycling pattern based on the last two digits is notoriously difficult to implement correctly in ICU MessageFormat.
linguisticChallenges.aspectAndPrefixes: Like Russian, Polish verbs have imperfective/perfective aspect pairs, but Polish adds extensive prefixal derivation that changes meaning — robić (do/make), zrobić (complete doing), dorobić (earn extra), wyrobić (produce), narobić (make a mess). MT must select the correct prefixed form.
linguisticChallenges.sevenCases: Polish has seven grammatical cases (adding vocative to the six Russian cases). The vocative is actively used in direct address — 'Panie Kowalski!' not 'Pan Kowalski!' in greetings. Case affects every noun, adjective, and numeral in the sentence.
linguisticChallenges.consonantClusters: Polish allows consonant clusters that are challenging for non-native speakers and can trip up text-to-speech systems — 'źdźbło' (blade of grass), 'szczęście' (happiness), 'bezwzględny' (ruthless). These are normal Polish and should not be simplified in translation.
linguisticChallenges.virileGender: Polish masculine has a virile/non-virile distinction in plural — human males use different adjective and verb forms than all other nouns (including female humans in mixed groups). 'Użytkownicy byli' (masculine-personal, users were) vs 'Pliki były' (non-personal, files were). MT frequently confuses these forms.

dialects.classification: Standard Polish based on the Greater Poland and Lesser Poland dialects. Relatively uniform; Silesian and Kashubian are sometimes classified as separate languages.

encyclopedic.history: Polish is the second most widely spoken Slavic language. It uses the Latin alphabet with extensive diacritics (ą, ć, ę, ł, ń, ó, ś, ź, ż). The language survived partition (1795–1918) when Poland was divided between Russia, Prussia, and Austria.

name: Portuguese (Brazilian)

formality.description: Brazilian Portuguese has a simplified T-V system compared to European Portuguese. 'Você' is the standard second-person pronoun for nearly all contexts — both formal and informal. 'Tu' is used in some regions (Rio Grande do Sul, parts of the North) but is not standard in most Brazilian writing. 'O senhor/A senhora' is reserved for very formal or elderly address. Software UI uses 'você' universally.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Portuguese has grammatical gender. For inclusive language, prefer gender-neutral restructuring where possible. The 'x' or 'e' endings (e.g., 'conectade', 'usuárix') are used in progressive contexts but are not standard. Slash notation (e.g., 'Conectado/a') is the safest inclusive approach for professional software.

professional.label: Profissional (Você)
professional.description: Standard for software UI, websites, and business communication in Brazil.
professional.prompt: Professional Brazilian Portuguese. Use você-form for second person. Clear, modern language appropriate for software interfaces and business communication. Avoid overly formal constructions.

formal.label: Formal (O senhor/A senhora)
formal.description: For banking, legal, government, or contexts addressing older demographics.
formal.prompt: Formal Brazilian Portuguese. Use 'o senhor' / 'a senhora' for respectful address. Elevated register for banking, legal, and government contexts. Precise vocabulary.

casual.label: Casual (Coloquial)
casual.description: For consumer apps, social media, and youth-oriented products.
casual.prompt: Casual Brazilian Portuguese. Conversational você-form. Warm, friendly tone with colloquial expressions where natural. For consumer apps, social media, and casual brand voices.

notes: This is Brazilian Portuguese — the default for the base 'pt' code. See pt-PT.json for European Portuguese, which has significant vocabulary and grammar differences.

linguisticChallenges.brPtDivergence: Brazilian and European Portuguese differ substantially in vocabulary, grammar, and spelling. 'Phone' is 'celular' (BR) vs 'telemóvel' (PT), 'bus' is 'ônibus' (BR) vs 'autocarro' (PT). The gerund/infinitive split is grammatical — BR uses 'estou fazendo' (gerund) while PT uses 'estou a fazer' (infinitive). MT must clearly target one variant.
linguisticChallenges.voceVsTu: Brazilian Portuguese pronouns are complicated — 'você' is standard everywhere but verb conjugation patterns differ by region. Some regions use 'tu' with 'você' verb forms (tu vai instead of tu vais). For software, standard 'você' with its correct conjugation (3rd person singular) is the safe choice.
linguisticChallenges.personalInfinitive: Portuguese has a personal (inflected) infinitive unique among Romance languages — 'para nós fazermos' (for us to do). This allows compact subordinate clauses but creates translation complexity. MT from English often misses opportunities to use it naturally, producing heavier subjunctive constructions instead.
linguisticChallenges.gerundVsInfinitive: This is the single most visible difference between BR and PT. 'Downloading...' → 'Baixando...' (BR, gerund) vs 'A transferir...' (PT, infinitive + preposition). Using the wrong form immediately flags the wrong regional variant.
linguisticChallenges.accentuation: Portuguese diacritics (á, â, ã, é, ê, í, ó, ô, õ, ú, ç) are mandatory and meaning-distinguishing. The 1990 Orthographic Agreement simplified some rules but remains controversial in Portugal. MT systems occasionally drop accents or use the pre-agreement spelling.
linguisticChallenges.cliticPlacement: Portuguese has complex clitic pronoun placement rules (proclisis, enclisis, mesoclisis). Brazilian Portuguese strongly prefers proclisis ('me diga' — tell me) while European Portuguese prefers enclisis ('diga-me'). MT systems sometimes produce mesoclisis ('dir-me-á') which sounds archaic even in European Portuguese.

dialects.classification: Brazilian Portuguese and European Portuguese are the two major standards. African varieties (Angola, Mozambique) generally follow European Portuguese. The 1990 Orthographic Agreement attempted partial unification.

encyclopedic.history: Portuguese spread globally during the Age of Exploration (15th-16th centuries). Brazil, with 215+ million people, accounts for over 80% of all Portuguese speakers, making Brazilian Portuguese the de facto global standard for digital products.

name: Portuguese (European)

formality.description: European Portuguese has a more complex T-V system than Brazilian. 'Tu' (informal), 'você' (semi-formal, though considered impolite in some regions), 'o senhor/a senhora' (formal). Portuguese software for Portugal typically uses tu-form or impersonal constructions. 'Você' is less common in Portugal than in Brazil.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Same rules as Brazilian Portuguese for gender-inclusive writing.

professional.label: Profissional (Professional)
professional.description: Standard for software UI targeting Portugal and European Portuguese speakers.
professional.prompt: Professional European Portuguese. Use tu-form or impersonal constructions (both are standard in Portuguese software for Portugal). European vocabulary — use 'ecrã' not 'tela', 'telemóvel' not 'celular'. Clear, modern language.

notes: European Portuguese has significant vocabulary and grammar differences from Brazilian Portuguese. This is not just a spelling variant.

linguisticChallenges.europeanVsBrazilian: European Portuguese differs substantially from Brazilian in vocabulary, grammar, and pronunciation. See pt.json for the full BR/PT divergence description.
linguisticChallenges.enclisis: European Portuguese strongly prefers enclisis (pronoun after verb: 'diga-me') while Brazilian prefers proclisis ('me diga'). This is the most visible grammatical difference.
linguisticChallenges.mesoclisis: European Portuguese still uses mesoclisis (pronoun inserted into future/conditional verbs: 'dir-lhe-ei' = I will tell him) in formal writing. This form is extinct in Brazilian Portuguese.
linguisticChallenges.orthographicAgreement: The 1990 Orthographic Agreement eliminated some spelling differences between BR and PT, but remains controversial in Portugal. Some words lost accents or consonants: 'acção' → 'ação', 'óptimo' → 'ótimo'.

dialects.classification: Standard European Portuguese based on the Lisbon-Coimbra dialect. Northern dialects differ in pronunciation.

encyclopedic.history: Portugal was the source of the language that spread globally during the Age of Exploration. European Portuguese retains more conservative features than Brazilian Portuguese, particularly in phonology.

name: Quechua

formality.description: Quechua does not have a grammaticalized T-V distinction. There is a single second-person pronoun (qam). Formality and respect are expressed through vocabulary choice, use of honorific suffixes (e.g., -lla for softening), and cultural context rather than pronoun alternation. Software UI uses standard, clear Quechua.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Quechua has no grammatical gender. Third-person pronouns (pay) are gender-neutral. No special inclusive conventions are needed.

professional.label: Allin Runasimi (Professional)
professional.description: Standard for software UI and institutional communication. Based on Southern Quechua (Cusco-Collao variety).
professional.prompt: Professional Quechua (Runasimi), Southern/Cusco-Collao variety. Clear, accessible language suitable for software interfaces. Use the standardized orthography with the five-vowel system (a, e, i, o, u). Include appropriate evidential suffixes — use -mi/-n for direct knowledge, -si/-s for reported information. Prefer pan-Quechua vocabulary where possible over highly local dialectal terms.

notes: Quechua is a macrolanguage (ISO 639-3 'que') comprising a family of related languages. This card represents Southern Quechua (Cusco-Collao variety), the most widely spoken and digitally resourced variety. Other major varieties include Ayacucho Quechua (quy), Ecuadorian Kichwa (qug), and Bolivian Quechua (quh). An orthography debate exists between three-vowel (a, i, u) and five-vowel (a, e, i, o, u) systems — this card uses the five-vowel system commonly used in Peru.

linguisticChallenges.evidentiality: Quechua grammatically encodes the source of information through evidential suffixes: -mi/-n (direct knowledge/assertion), -si/-s (hearsay/reported), -chá (inference/conjecture). English has no equivalent system — MT must choose the correct evidential for every statement, and the wrong choice can imply the speaker is lying or uncertain when they aren't.
linguisticChallenges.agglutination: Quechua is highly agglutinative — a single word can encode what English expresses in an entire clause. For example, 'wasiykiraqchismá' means approximately 'I wonder if it might still be your house'. MT must correctly stack suffixes in the right order.
linguisticChallenges.inclusiveExclusiveWe: Quechua distinguishes between inclusive 'we' (ñuqanchis — you and I) and exclusive 'we' (ñuqayku — us but not you). English 'we' is ambiguous, so MT must infer which form is appropriate from context — the wrong choice changes meaning significantly.
linguisticChallenges.dialectalVariation: Quechua varieties differ substantially — Cusco, Ayacucho, Ecuadorian Kichwa, and Bolivian Quechua are partially mutually unintelligible. MT training data mixes varieties, causing inconsistent output. This card targets Southern Quechua (Cusco-Collao).
linguisticChallenges.limitedDigitalResources: Quechua has very limited parallel corpora and digital text. MT models have sparse training data, leading to lower quality than well-resourced languages. Code-mixing with Spanish is extremely common in practice but undesirable in formal translation.
linguisticChallenges.orthographyDebate: Quechua has competing orthographic standards — the Academia Mayor uses a five-vowel system (a, e, i, o, u) while linguists prefer a three-vowel system (a, i, u) that better represents Quechua phonology. MT output may mix conventions.

formality.description: Romanian has a T-V distinction: dumneavoastră (formal, literally 'your lordship') vs tu (informal). Professional software typically uses dumneavoastră. Some modern consumer software uses the intermediate 'voi' (you plural) or directly 'tu'.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Romanian has grammatical gender (masculine, feminine, neuter). When gender is unknown, masculine plural is the conventional default.

formal-dvs.label: Dumneavoastră (Formal)
formal-dvs.description: Standard for professional software, enterprise, and institutional contexts.
formal-dvs.prompt: Formal Romanian. Use dumneavoastră for second-person address. Professional register for software interfaces.

informal-tu.label: Tu (Informal)
informal-tu.description: For consumer apps, social media, and casual contexts.
informal-tu.prompt: Informal Romanian. Use tu-form. Conversational tone for consumer products.

linguisticChallenges.definiteSuffix: Romanian marks definiteness with a suffix (-ul/-le, -a, -lui) attached to the noun, similar to Bulgarian. MT must produce suffixed articles, not separate words.
linguisticChallenges.caseSystem: Romanian retains a case system (unique among Romance languages) with nominative-accusative and genitive-dative distinctions.
linguisticChallenges.diacritics: Romanian uses ș, ț, â, î, ă. Missing diacritics change meaning — 'tara' vs 'țara' (country). Old cedilla forms (ş, ţ) vs comma-below forms (ș, ț) create font/encoding issues.

dialects.classification: Standard Romanian based on Wallachian dialect. Moldovan is politically distinct but linguistically identical.

encyclopedic.history: Romanian is the only Romance language in Eastern Europe, surrounded by Slavic and Hungarian languages. It retained Latin grammar structure despite heavy Slavic vocabulary influence.

formality.description: Russian has a clear T-V distinction: вы (vy, formal/plural) vs ты (ty, informal/singular). Professional software uses Вы-form (often capitalized for single addressee in writing). The formal register uses longer sentence constructions and avoids colloquialisms.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Russian has grammatical gender on nouns, adjectives, and past-tense verbs. There is no widely adopted gender-neutral writing convention. When gender is unknown, masculine is the conventional default. Where possible, restructure to avoid gendered forms.

formal-vy.label: Вы (Formal)
formal-vy.description: Standard for professional software, enterprise, and institutional contexts.
formal-vy.prompt: Formal Russian. Use Вы-form (capitalized for single addressee). Professional register appropriate for software interfaces, enterprise, and business communication. Avoid colloquialisms and informal expressions.

informal-ty.label: Ты (Informal)
informal-ty.description: For consumer apps, social media, gaming, and casual brand voices.
informal-ty.prompt: Informal Russian. Use ты-form. Conversational, friendly tone for consumer products, social media, and gaming. Colloquial expressions acceptable when natural.

linguisticChallenges.aspectPairs: Russian verbs come in imperfective/perfective aspect pairs (делать/сделать, читать/прочитать). Choosing the wrong aspect changes meaning subtly but significantly — 'Файл загружается' (imperfective, loading in progress) vs 'Файл загрузился' (perfective, loading completed). MT systems frequently select the wrong aspect, especially for UI state descriptions.
linguisticChallenges.complexCaseSystem: Russian has six grammatical cases (nominative, genitive, dative, accusative, instrumental, prepositional) that affect noun, adjective, and pronoun endings. Placeholder variables create particular challenges because the case of the interpolated noun depends on its syntactic role — '{file} удалён' (nominative) vs 'Нет {file}' (genitive).
linguisticChallenges.vyCapitalization: Formal Вы (capitalized) for single addressee vs lowercase вы (plural or generic). In software UI, Вы is conventional for formal register, but inconsistent capitalization between screens reads as sloppy. MT models are inconsistent about this capitalization.
linguisticChallenges.verbsOfMotion: Russian has a complex system of motion verbs with determined/indetermined pairs (идти/ходить, ехать/ездить) and numerous prefixed derivatives (выходить, входить, уходить, приходить...). Each prefix changes the meaning. MT frequently selects the wrong motion verb, especially for metaphorical uses.
linguisticChallenges.genderedPastTense: Russian past-tense verbs agree in gender with their subject — 'Вы подключились' (masculine) vs 'Вы подключились' (same spelling but different pronunciation) vs 'Вы подключилась' (feminine, informal ты). This creates the same unknown-gender problem as other gendered languages, but it appears in the verb rather than adjectives.
linguisticChallenges.wordOrderFlexibility: Russian word order is flexible (SVO is default but all permutations are grammatical) because case endings disambiguate meaning. Different orders carry different emphases. MT from English tends to produce rigid SVO, which sounds unnatural in contexts where Russian would front-load the topic.

dialects.classification: Standard Russian based on Moscow dialect. Relatively uniform across the vast geographic range. Ukrainian and Belarusian are separate languages, not dialects.

encyclopedic.history: Russian became a standardized literary language in the 18th century under Peter the Great's modernization. The 1918 spelling reform simplified the orthography after the Russian Revolution. It is one of the six official UN languages.

name: Serbian

formality.description: Serbian has a T-V distinction: Ви (Vi, formal/plural) vs ти (ti, informal/singular). Professional software uses Ви-form. Serbian is written in both Cyrillic (official) and Latin scripts — rosetta translates to Latin script and offers a deterministic Cyrillic converter.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Serbian has grammatical gender. When gender is unknown, masculine is the conventional default.

formal-vi.label: Ви (Formal)
formal-vi.description: Standard for professional software. Output in Latin script, optionally converted to Cyrillic.
formal-vi.prompt: Formal Serbian. Use Ви-form for second-person address. Professional register. Translate in Latin script — Cyrillic conversion is handled automatically by the script converter if enabled.

informal-ti.label: Ти (Informal)
informal-ti.description: For consumer apps and casual contexts.
informal-ti.prompt: Informal Serbian. Use ти-form. Conversational tone. Translate in Latin script.

notes: Serbian uses both Latin and Cyrillic scripts interchangeably. The translation pipeline works in Latin, then the deterministic Latin→Cyrillic converter (scripts.js) can produce the Cyrillic variant.

linguisticChallenges.dualScript: Serbian uses both Cyrillic (official) and Latin script interchangeably. Software must support both or clearly target one. MT output must be in the correct script — producing Cyrillic when Latin is expected (or vice versa) is a critical error.
linguisticChallenges.sevenCases: Serbian has seven grammatical cases like other South Slavic languages, with complex noun and adjective declension.
linguisticChallenges.ekavianVsIjekavian: Serbian has two pronunciation/spelling standards: Ekavian (Belgrade, used in Serbia) and Ijekavian (used in Bosnia, Montenegro). 'Mleko' vs 'Mlijeko' (milk). Software typically targets Ekavian.

dialects.classification: Standard Serbian uses Ekavian pronunciation (Belgrade dialect). Ijekavian is used in western regions. Bosnian and Croatian are near-identical but politically distinct.

encyclopedic.history: Serbian, Croatian, Bosnian, and Montenegrin were historically considered one language (Serbo-Croatian). Political separation after Yugoslavia's dissolution led to distinct standardization, though mutual intelligibility remains near-total.

name: Shakespearean English

formality.description: Constructed language or stylistic variant — no formality system. Tone is defined entirely by the register preset.

standard.label: Standard Shakespearean
standard.description: Early Modern English with thee/thou forms and period-appropriate vocabulary.
standard.prompt: Shakespearean English. Use Early Modern English conventions: thee/thou for second person, -eth/-est verb endings (e.g., 'thou hast', 'it doth'), period-appropriate vocabulary. Maintain comprehensibility — the user should understand the meaning even if the style is archaic. UI labels can be shorter; descriptions get the full treatment.

notes: Register-only conlang — English with Early Modern English style transformation.

name: Sindarin (Tolkien Elvish)

formality.description: Sindarin is J.R.R. Tolkien's Elvish language. It has a formal/informal distinction through verb mutation and pronoun choice, but the corpus is small enough that only a general register is practical for translation. Sindarin uses Latin script for working translation; Tengwar script is available via converter.

standard.label: Standard Sindarin
standard.description: General Sindarin following published Tolkien sources and Neo-Sindarin conventions.
standard.prompt: Sindarin (Tolkien Elvish). Use vocabulary from published Tolkien sources (The Lord of the Rings, The Silmarillion, History of Middle-earth). For technical terms not in Tolkien's corpus, use established Neo-Sindarin coinages from peer-reviewed fan scholarship. Translate in Latin script — Tengwar conversion is handled by the script converter.

notes: Tengwar script requires a CSUR-compatible font (PUA U+E000–E07F). Mode of Beleriand is used for the deterministic converter.

formality.description: Slovak has a T-V distinction: vy (vykanie, formal) vs ty (tykanie, informal). Very similar to Czech. Professional software uses vy-form.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Slovak has grammatical gender. When gender is unknown, masculine is the conventional default.

formal-vy.label: Vykanie (Formal)
formal-vy.description: Standard for professional software and business communication.
formal-vy.prompt: Formal Slovak. Use vykanie (vy-form) consistently. Professional register for software interfaces.

informal-ty.label: Tykanie (Informal)
informal-ty.description: For consumer apps, social media, and casual contexts.
informal-ty.prompt: Informal Slovak. Use tykanie (ty-form). Conversational tone for consumer products.

linguisticChallenges.closeToCzech: Slovak and Czech are closely related and partially mutually intelligible. MT models sometimes produce Czech words in Slovak output or vice versa. Key vocabulary differences exist — 'vlak' (SK, train) vs 'vlak' (CS, same but different compounds).
linguisticChallenges.sevenCases: Like Czech and Polish, Slovak has seven grammatical cases with complex declension patterns.
linguisticChallenges.rhythmicRule: Slovak has a unique 'rhythmic law' (rytmický zákon) where two consecutive long syllables are prohibited. This affects suffix forms and can trip up MT models that don't encode this phonological rule.

dialects.classification: Standard Slovak based on Central Slovak dialects. Western dialects are transitional toward Czech.

encyclopedic.history: Slovak was standardized in the 19th century by Ľudovít Štúr based on Central Slovak dialects, distinguishing it from the earlier Czech-based literary tradition.

name: Spanish

formality.description: Spanish has a complex T-V system that varies by region. 'Tú' (informal) vs 'usted' (formal) is universal, but Latin American Spanish also uses 'vos' (rioplatense, Central American). The default code 'es' targets neutral Latin American Spanish — formal enough for professional contexts but avoiding distinctly Peninsular vocabulary (e.g., 'ordenador' → 'computadora'). Regional variants (es-MX, es-AR) can override.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Spanish has grammatical gender. For inclusive language, avoid gendered defaults where possible: prefer role-neutral terms (e.g., 'persona usuaria' or 'quien usa' over 'usuario/a'). The 'x' and 'e' inclusive endings (e.g., 'usuarie', 'todxs') remain controversial — use them only if the product explicitly targets progressive audiences.

neutral-latam.label: Neutro (Latin American)
neutral-latam.description: Standard for international Spanish-language products. Neutral Latin American vocabulary, avoids region-specific slang.
neutral-latam.prompt: Neutral Latin American Spanish. Professional register using usted-form for direct address. Avoid Peninsular-specific vocabulary (use 'computadora' not 'ordenador', 'celular' not 'móvil').

formal-usted.label: Formal (Usted)
formal-usted.description: For government, legal, banking, and enterprise software across all regions.
formal-usted.prompt: Formal Spanish. Strict usted-form. Elevated register appropriate for legal, government, financial, and enterprise contexts. Precise vocabulary, no colloquialisms.

informal-tu.label: Informal (Tú)
informal-tu.description: For consumer apps, gaming, social media with a warm, direct tone.
informal-tu.prompt: Informal Spanish. Use tú-form. Warm, conversational tone for consumer products. Neutral Latin American vocabulary. Colloquial expressions acceptable when natural.

notes: The base 'es' code targets neutral Latin American Spanish. See es-MX.json for Mexico-specific variant.

linguisticChallenges.regionalVocabularyDivergence: Spanish has the largest regional vocabulary split of any European language. 'Computer' alone has four variants: computadora (LatAm), ordenador (Spain), computador (Colombia/Chile). MT must target a specific region or use neutral vocabulary. A single Peninsular term in a LatAm product immediately signals 'wrong Spanish'.
linguisticChallenges.voseo: Several Latin American countries (Argentina, Uruguay, parts of Central America) use 'vos' instead of 'tú' for informal address, with different verb conjugations (vos tenés vs tú tienes). MT models default to tú unless explicitly prompted. Using tú in Argentina reads as foreign.
linguisticChallenges.invertedPunctuation: Spanish requires inverted question marks (¿) and exclamation marks (¡) at the beginning of interrogative/exclamatory clauses. MT systems almost always produce these correctly, but they occasionally omit them or misplace them in complex sentences. Their absence is immediately noticeable to native readers.
linguisticChallenges.subjunctiveMood: Spanish has a productive subjunctive mood used in wishes, doubts, hypotheticals, and subordinate clauses. English doesn't map cleanly onto these uses. MT frequently produces indicative where subjunctive is required (e.g., 'Esperamos que *tiene' instead of 'Esperamos que tenga'), which sounds grammatically wrong.
linguisticChallenges.genderedInclusivity: The -e ending (e.g., 'todes' instead of 'todos') and the -x ending ('todxs') for gender-inclusive Spanish are contested. The Real Academia Española opposes both. Usage varies strongly by region and audience. MT models almost never produce these forms without explicit instruction.
linguisticChallenges.diminutivesAndAugmentatives: Spanish uses productive diminutive (-ito/-ita, -illo/-illa) and augmentative (-ón/-ona, -azo/-aza) suffixes that convey affection, smallness, emphasis, or disdain. These are common in casual speech but should generally be avoided in professional UI text. MT sometimes produces them inappropriately.

dialects.classification: Major dialect groups: Peninsular (Castilian), Mexican, Central American, Caribbean, Andean, Rioplatense. Mutual intelligibility is high but vocabulary and pronunciation differ significantly.

encyclopedic.history: Spanish is the world's fourth most spoken language and the second most spoken by native speakers. It spread to the Americas through colonization starting in 1492. The Real Academia Española (RAE, founded 1713) regulates the language alongside 22 national academies.

formality.description: Swahili does not have a T-V pronoun distinction. 'Wewe' (you singular) is universal. Formality is expressed through vocabulary choice, use of honorifics (Bwana/Bibi), and register. The Swahili noun class system affects agreement patterns but does not encode formality.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Swahili uses a noun class system rather than grammatical gender. Pronouns do not distinguish gender.

professional.label: Rasmi (Professional)
professional.description: Standard for software UI and business communication.
professional.prompt: Professional Swahili (Kiswahili sanifu). Modern standard Swahili appropriate for software interfaces and business communication. Clear, accessible vocabulary.

linguisticChallenges.nounClassSystem: Swahili has 15-18 noun classes (similar to grammatical gender but far more complex). Each class takes different prefixes on nouns, adjectives, verbs, and demonstratives. MT must maintain agreement across all these elements.
linguisticChallenges.agglutination: Swahili verbs encode subject, tense, object, and other information in prefixes and suffixes: 'nitakupenda' = ni-ta-ku-penda (I-will-you-love). MT must correctly sequence these morphemes.
linguisticChallenges.loanwordLayers: Swahili has substantial Arabic and English loanword layers. Technical terms often come from English — the balance between native Swahili and English terms varies by register.

dialects.classification: Standard Swahili based on the Zanzibar (Unguja) dialect. Used as a lingua franca across East Africa.

encyclopedic.history: Swahili is the most widely spoken Bantu language and serves as a lingua franca across East Africa. It was adopted by the African Union as an official language in 2004.

formality.description: Swedish historically had a complex formality system but underwent the 'du-reform' (du-reformen) in the 1960s–70s. Modern Swedish uses 'du' universally. 'Ni' as formal address is sometimes used in retail/service but is not standard in software.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Swedish has common and neuter genders (en/ett). The gender-neutral pronoun 'hen' is officially recognized and widely accepted.

professional.label: Professionell (Professional)
professional.description: Standard for software UI and business communication.
professional.prompt: Professional Swedish. Use du-form (universal in modern Swedish). Clear, modern language.

linguisticChallenges.enEttGender: Swedish has two grammatical genders: common (en-words) and neuter (ett-words). Like Danish, wrong article choice is conspicuous — 'en dator' (a computer) vs 'ett program' (a program).
linguisticChallenges.compoundNouns: Swedish forms compounds by concatenation. 'Sjukhus' (hospital = sick + house), 'datorprogram' (computer program). Linking rules (-s-, -e-) must be correct.
linguisticChallenges.duVsNi: Swedish underwent a pronoun reform in the 1960s-70s where the informal 'du' largely replaced the formal 'ni'. Modern Swedish software uses 'du' universally — using 'ni' sounds old-fashioned or ironically formal.
linguisticChallenges.scandinavianMixing: MT models sometimes produce Norwegian or Danish words in Swedish output due to the high mutual intelligibility. 'Ikke' (NO/DA) instead of 'inte' (SV), for example.

dialects.classification: Standard Swedish (rikssvenska) based on Central Swedish dialects. Finland Swedish is a recognized variety with some vocabulary differences.

encyclopedic.history: Swedish is an official language in both Sweden and Finland. The 'du-reform' of the late 1960s democratized address forms, making Swedish one of the most egalitarian European languages in terms of formality.

name: Thai

formality.description: Thai formality is expressed through politeness particles appended to sentences and pronoun choice. The particles ครับ (khrap, male speaker) and ค่ะ/คะ (kha, female speaker) indicate politeness but are gendered to the speaker, not the listener. Software UI conventionally omits these particles entirely to maintain gender neutrality. Formality is also expressed through pronoun choice: ผม (phom, formal male I), ดิฉัน (dichan, formal female I), เรา (rao, we/neutral I), คุณ (khun, you/neutral).

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Thai politeness particles ครับ (male) and ค่ะ/คะ (female) are gendered to the speaker. Software UI should omit particles entirely — this is standard practice and does not read as rude. Use เรา (we/neutral) rather than gendered first-person pronouns. Use คุณ (khun) for second person.

neutral-professional.label: เป็นทางการ (Professional, no particles)
neutral-professional.description: Standard for software UI. Omits gendered particles. Clean and neutral.
neutral-professional.prompt: Professional Thai. Use คุณ (khun) for second person, เรา (rao) for first person when needed. Clear, concise phrasing appropriate for digital interfaces.

polite-male.label: สุภาพ ครับ (Polite, male voice)
polite-male.description: For chatbots, virtual assistants, or personas with a male-presenting voice.
polite-male.prompt: Polite Thai with ครับ (khrap) particle. For contexts where a male-presenting voice is appropriate — chatbots, customer service with male persona, conversational UI. Use ผม (phom) for first person.

polite-female.label: สุภาพ ค่ะ (Polite, female voice)
polite-female.description: For chatbots, virtual assistants, or personas with a female-presenting voice.
polite-female.prompt: Polite Thai with ค่ะ/คะ (kha) particles. For contexts where a female-presenting voice is appropriate — chatbots, customer service with female persona, conversational UI. Use ดิฉัน (dichan) for first person.

notes: Previous default included gendered particles which was problematic. Changed to particle-free professional register as the default.

linguisticChallenges.wordSegmentation: Thai has no spaces between words — spaces only appear between sentences or clauses. Word boundaries are ambiguous and require dictionary-based or statistical segmentation. This makes placeholder insertion in translation strings particularly challenging, as the system must determine where word boundaries fall around variables.
linguisticChallenges.tones: Thai has five tones (mid, low, falling, high, rising) that distinguish meaning. While tones don't directly affect written translation, MT systems trained primarily on text may produce output with incorrect tonal marker placement when diacritics are needed, and homophone confusion between tonal minimal pairs is common.
linguisticChallenges.classifiers: Thai uses classifiers (ลักษณนาม) between numbers and nouns, similar to Chinese. Using the wrong classifier (e.g., ตัว for animals vs คน for people vs เครื่อง for machines) sounds immediately wrong. The generic classifier อัน is acceptable for unfamiliar nouns.
linguisticChallenges.genderedParticles: The politeness particles ครับ (male) and ค่ะ/คะ (female) are gendered to the speaker. Software UI should omit them entirely — this is the industry standard and does not read as impolite. MT systems often insert them, which forces an assumption about the user's gender.
linguisticChallenges.royalVocabulary: Thai has a completely separate royal vocabulary (ราชาศัพท์) used when referring to the monarchy. Using royal terms in non-royal contexts is a serious social error, and using common terms in royal contexts is equally problematic. MT must never produce royal vocabulary in standard UI text.
linguisticChallenges.serialVerbs: Thai uses serial verb constructions where multiple verbs chain together without conjunctions. English compound sentences often translate to serial verb chains, and MT systems sometimes over-conjoin them with 'และ' (and), producing stilted output.

dialects.classification: Standard Thai based on Central Thai (Bangkok dialect); regional dialects (Isan, Northern, Southern) exist but are not used in software

encyclopedic.history: Thai script was created in 1283 by King Ramkhamhaeng, adapted from the Khmer script. It is an abugida where consonant-vowel combinations are written as single units, with vowels appearing before, after, above, or below their consonant.

dictionaries.name: Royal Institute Dictionary (พจนานุกรมฉบับราชบัณฑิตยสถาน)

formality.description: Turkish has a T-V distinction: siz (formal/plural) vs sen (informal/singular). Professional software uses siz-form. Turkish is agglutinative, so formality also appears in verb suffixes (-siniz vs -sin).

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Turkish has no grammatical gender. The third-person pronoun 'o' is gender-neutral.

formal-siz.label: Siz (Formal)
formal-siz.description: Standard for professional software, enterprise, and institutional contexts.
formal-siz.prompt: Formal Turkish. Use siz-form consistently. Professional register appropriate for software interfaces and business communication. Clear, precise vocabulary. Avoid colloquialisms.

informal-sen.label: Sen (Informal)
informal-sen.description: For consumer apps, social media, gaming, and youth-oriented products.
informal-sen.prompt: Informal Turkish. Use sen-form. Warm, conversational tone for consumer products and social platforms. Colloquial expressions acceptable.

linguisticChallenges.agglutination: Turkish is heavily agglutinative — a single word can contain the root, tense, mood, person, negation, and question markers: 'yapabilecekmiydik' (were we going to be able to do). MT systems struggle with complex agglutinated forms and sometimes break words at incorrect morpheme boundaries.
linguisticChallenges.vowelHarmony: Turkish has strict vowel harmony — suffixes change their vowels to match the preceding syllable (front/back, rounded/unrounded). Breaking vowel harmony ('geliyorlar' is correct, not 'geliyorler') is immediately noticeable and marks output as non-native.
linguisticChallenges.softeningAndBufferConsonants: Consonant mutations (k→ğ, t→d, p→b, ç→c) and buffer consonants (y, n, s) apply when adding suffixes to stems. Getting these wrong produces misspellings — 'kitabı' (his book) not 'kitapı'. MT models frequently miss these mutations.
linguisticChallenges.definiteAccusative: Turkish marks definite direct objects with the accusative suffix -(y)ı/-(y)i: 'Dosyayı sil' (delete THE file) vs 'Dosya sil' (delete A file). English doesn't distinguish these, so MT from English often omits the accusative when it's required.
linguisticChallenges.noGrammaticalGender: Turkish has no grammatical gender at all — 'o' means he/she/it. This is an advantage for gender-neutral UI, but translating FROM Turkish to gendered languages requires context-based gender inference.
linguisticChallenges.wordOrderSOV: Turkish is SOV with the verb at the end. Variables in translation strings must account for this — English '{user} deleted {file}' → '{user} {file} dosyasını sildi'. The verb always comes last.

dialects.classification: Standard Turkish based on Istanbul dialect. Relatively uniform; Azerbaijani and Turkmen are separate languages.

encyclopedic.history: Modern Turkish was reformed by Atatürk in 1928 when the Arabic script was replaced with a modified Latin alphabet. The Turkish Language Association (TDK) was founded in 1932 to purify the language of Arabic and Persian loanwords.

name: Ukrainian

formality.description: Ukrainian has a T-V distinction: Ви (vy, formal/plural) vs ти (ty, informal/singular). Professional software uses Ви-form (capitalized for single addressee). Similar to Russian but with distinct vocabulary and grammar.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Ukrainian has grammatical gender. When gender is unknown, masculine is the conventional default. Where possible, restructure to use gender-neutral constructions.

formal-vy.label: Ви (Formal)
formal-vy.description: Standard for professional software, enterprise, and institutional contexts.
formal-vy.prompt: Formal Ukrainian. Use Ви-form (capitalized for single addressee). Professional register appropriate for software interfaces and business communication. Avoid Russian loanwords — use Ukrainian equivalents.

informal-ty.label: Ти (Informal)
informal-ty.description: For consumer apps, social media, gaming, and casual brand voices.
informal-ty.prompt: Informal Ukrainian. Use ти-form. Conversational tone for consumer products. Avoid Russian loanwords — use Ukrainian equivalents.

notes: Ukrainian and Russian are distinct languages. Both registers explicitly note avoiding Russian loanwords in favor of Ukrainian equivalents — this is both linguistically correct and culturally important.

linguisticChallenges.ukrainianVsRussian: Ukrainian and Russian are distinct languages despite superficial similarity. MT models sometimes produce Russian words in Ukrainian output (especially for technical terms). Key differences: 'и' (UA) vs 'и' (RU) are different sounds; 'ґ' exists only in Ukrainian.
linguisticChallenges.sevenCases: Ukrainian has seven grammatical cases like other East Slavic languages. The vocative case is actively used in modern Ukrainian, unlike in Russian.
linguisticChallenges.apostrophe: Ukrainian uses the apostrophe (') as a letter to indicate non-palatalization before iotated vowels: 'м'який' (soft). This is a meaningful orthographic element, not punctuation.

dialects.classification: Standard Ukrainian based on the southeastern dialect. Northern, southwestern, and southeastern dialect groups exist.

encyclopedic.history: Ukrainian is the second most spoken Slavic language. Its use was restricted during both Russian Imperial and Soviet periods. Since 2019, Ukrainian is the sole state language of Ukraine.

name: Urdu

formality.description: Urdu has a three-tier pronoun system similar to Hindi: آپ (āp, formal/respectful), تم (tum, familiar), and تو (tū, intimate/condescending). Software UI uses آپ consistently. Urdu uses Nastaliq script (a calligraphic style of Arabic script), though Naskh is acceptable for digital contexts.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Urdu verbs and adjectives agree in gender. When gender is unknown, masculine is the conventional default.

formal-aap.label: آپ (Formal)
formal-aap.description: Standard for professional software and business communication.
formal-aap.prompt: Formal Urdu. Use آپ (āp) for second-person address. Professional register. RTL layout. Modern standard Urdu appropriate for software interfaces.

informal-tum.label: تم (Informal)
informal-tum.description: For consumer apps, social media, and casual contexts.
informal-tum.prompt: Informal Urdu. Use تم (tum) form. Conversational tone for consumer products. RTL layout.

notes: Urdu and Hindi are mutually intelligible in spoken form but use different scripts (Urdu: Nastaliq/Arabic, Hindi: Devanagari) and draw from different literary traditions (Urdu: Persian/Arabic, Hindi: Sanskrit).

linguisticChallenges.nastaliqScript: Urdu uses the Nastaliq calligraphic style of the Perso-Arabic script, which is more complex to render than Naskh (used for Arabic). Many digital fonts default to Naskh, producing readable but aesthetically wrong Urdu.
linguisticChallenges.hindiUrduContinuum: Urdu and Hindi are mutually intelligible in spoken form but use different scripts and draw vocabulary from different sources (Persian/Arabic for Urdu vs Sanskrit for Hindi). MT models sometimes produce Hindi-influenced vocabulary.
linguisticChallenges.rtlWithMixedScript: Like Arabic and Persian, Urdu faces bidi challenges with embedded LTR content.

dialects.classification: Standard Urdu based on the Khariboli dialect (same base as Hindi). Register differences come from vocabulary source (Persian/Arabic vs Sanskrit).

encyclopedic.history: Urdu developed as a literary register of Hindustani during the Mughal period, drawing heavily on Persian and Arabic vocabulary. It became the national language of Pakistan at independence in 1947.

name: Vietnamese

formality.description: Vietnamese formality is expressed almost entirely through pronoun choice. There is no verb conjugation for formality. The pronoun system encodes age, gender, and social relationship: anh/chị (older brother/sister, common polite), em (younger person), bạn (friend/peer, neutral), quý vị (distinguished, very formal), tôi (I, neutral formal). Software UI typically uses bạn (neutral, peer-like) for second person, which is friendly without being presumptuous.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Vietnamese pronouns encode perceived age and gender relative to the speaker (anh = older male, chị = older female). Software UI should use bạn (gender-neutral peer) to avoid assumptions about user age and gender.

neutral-ban.label: Bạn (Neutral)
neutral-ban.description: Standard for software UI. Uses bạn (neutral peer) for second person. Friendly and appropriate for most digital products.
neutral-ban.prompt: Professional Vietnamese. Use bạn for second person (neutral, peer-level — appropriate for software UI). Use tôi for first person when needed. Clear, modern Vietnamese appropriate for digital interfaces. Avoid overly formal constructions.

formal-quyvi.label: Quý vị (Formal)
formal-quyvi.description: For government, banking, legal, or contexts addressing a general public audience with respect.
formal-quyvi.prompt: Formal Vietnamese. Use quý vị for second-person plural/respectful address, quý khách for customer-facing contexts. Elevated register appropriate for government, banking, and official communications.

notes: Previous default had no pronoun guidance. Added explicit bạn/quý vị pronoun choice which is critical for appropriate Vietnamese translation.

linguisticChallenges.pronounSystemComplexity: Vietnamese has dozens of pronouns encoding age, gender, and social relationship relative to the speaker. Using the wrong pronoun (e.g., anh for an older person when they're younger) is a social error. Software UI should use bạn (gender-neutral peer) to sidestep this entirely, but MT systems often produce more specific pronouns calqued from English 'you'.
linguisticChallenges.tonalDiacritics: Vietnamese uses six tones marked by diacritics (à á ả ã ạ + unmarked). Missing or incorrect diacritics change meaning — 'ma' means ghost, 'má' means mother, 'mã' means horse, 'mạ' means rice seedling. MT systems must produce correct diacritics or the output is wrong, not merely informal.
linguisticChallenges.classifierSystem: Vietnamese uses classifiers (loại từ) between numbers and nouns — con (animals), cái (inanimate objects), chiếc (single items of a pair), quyển (books). Omitting classifiers is grammatically wrong, and using the wrong one sounds uneducated.
linguisticChallenges.sinoVietnameseVsNative: Vietnamese vocabulary has a Sino-Vietnamese layer (from Chinese) and a native layer. Sino-Vietnamese words are more formal (e.g., 'hỏa hoạn' vs 'cháy' for fire). MT must calibrate between these layers based on register, similar to the Latin/Germanic split in English.
linguisticChallenges.noMorphologicalInflection: Vietnamese has no verb conjugation, no noun declension, no plural markers, and no grammatical gender agreement. Tense and aspect are expressed through adverbs and context. MT from heavily inflected languages sometimes produces redundant temporal markers.
linguisticChallenges.compoundWords: Vietnamese compound words are multi-syllable but each syllable is written as a separate word with spaces. This makes tokenization ambiguous — 'chủ tịch' (chairman) is two words but one concept. MT systems sometimes split compounds inappropriately.

dialects.classification: Three main dialect groups (Northern/Hanoi, Central/Hue, Southern/Saigon) — mutually intelligible but differ in pronunciation and some vocabulary. Standard Vietnamese is based on the Hanoi dialect.

encyclopedic.history: Vietnamese switched from Chinese characters (chữ Nôm) to a Latin-based alphabet (chữ Quốc ngữ) in the early 20th century. The romanization was originally created by Portuguese missionaries in the 17th century and later standardized by French colonists.

name: Yoda-speak

formality.description: Constructed language or stylistic variant — no formality system. Tone is defined entirely by the register preset.

standard.label: Standard Yoda
standard.description: OSV word order with Yoda's characteristic syntax inversion.
standard.prompt: Yoda-speak. Rearrange English sentences into Yoda's characteristic OSV (Object-Subject-Verb) word order. 'The file was saved' becomes 'Saved, the file was.' Keep meaning clear despite syntax inversion. Add characteristic Yoda expressions ('hmm', 'yes') sparingly. Short UI labels may not need inversion if it would be incomprehensible.

notes: Register-only conlang — English with Yoda's OSV syntax transformation.

name: Yoruba

formality.description: Yoruba expresses formality through vocabulary, greetings, and pronoun usage rather than a strict T-V system. The pronoun ẹ (second person plural/respectful) is used for elders and authority figures, while ìwọ/o is informal. Formality is also marked through extensive greeting protocols and honorific titles (e.g., using profession or kinship titles). Software UI typically uses the respectful ẹ-form.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Yoruba has no grammatical gender. Third-person pronouns (ó/oun) are gender-neutral. No special inclusive conventions are needed.

professional.label: Ọ̀rọ̀ ìṣòwò (Professional)
professional.description: Standard for software UI, websites, and business communication.
professional.prompt: Professional Yoruba. Use ẹ-form for second-person address (respectful/plural). Standard Yoruba with proper tonal diacritics on all vowels. Avoid dialectal forms — use the standard literary Yoruba based on Ọ̀yọ́ dialect. All tonal marks must be present for clarity.

casual.label: Èdè àìṣòtítọ́ (Casual)
casual.description: For consumer apps, social media, and casual contexts.
casual.prompt: Casual Yoruba. Use ìwọ/o for second-person address. Conversational tone, modern vocabulary. Tonal diacritics should still be present for disambiguation. Natural conversational Yoruba for digital contexts.

notes: Yoruba is a tonal language with three tones (high, mid, low). Proper diacritics are critical for disambiguation — e.g., 'ọkọ' can mean 'husband', 'hoe', 'vehicle', or 'spear' depending on tone. Many informal digital texts omit diacritics, but MT output should always include them. Yoruba pluralization uses àwọn (a determiner) rather than noun morphology.

linguisticChallenges.tonalDiacritics: Yoruba has three lexical tones (high ´, mid unmarked, low `) and two tone-modifying processes (rising, falling). Tonal diacritics are essential for disambiguation — 'ọkọ' without diacritics could mean husband, hoe, vehicle, or spear. MT must always produce properly marked output even though informal digital Yoruba often omits them.
linguisticChallenges.serialVerbConstructions: Yoruba extensively uses serial verb constructions where multiple verbs share a subject without conjunctions (e.g., 'Mo mú ìwé wá' — I took book came = 'I brought a book'). MT from English single-verb constructions often fails to produce natural serial verb sequences.
linguisticChallenges.ideophones: Yoruba has a rich system of ideophones (sound-symbolic words describing sensory experiences) that don't translate literally. These are integral to natural expression, not optional embellishments. MT typically either omits them or produces awkward literal equivalents.
linguisticChallenges.subDotDiacritics: Yoruba uses sub-dot diacritics (ẹ, ọ, ṣ) that are distinct phonemes, not decorative marks. ẹ ≠ e, ọ ≠ o, ṣ ≠ s. MT systems sometimes drop or misplace these, producing words with different meanings.
linguisticChallenges.pluralDeterminer: Yoruba marks plurality with the determiner àwọn rather than noun morphology. English plural nouns must be mapped to àwọn + singular noun constructions. MT often incorrectly attempts morphological pluralization.
linguisticChallenges.relativeClausePronoun: Yoruba relative clauses use the particle tí, and complex sentence embedding follows different rules than English. Direct structural translation of English relative clauses often produces unnatural or ambiguous Yoruba.
